questions.specification: A shipping crate of mass $$24\text{ kg}$$ is being pulled along a horizontal warehouse floor by a nylon strap. The strap is held at an angle of $$32^\circ$$ above the horizontal. The tension in the strap is maintained at $$110\text{ N}$$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.2 Forces and momentum > A.2.1 Newton's Laws of Motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.1 Newton's Laws of Motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses force components and vertical equilibrium to find the normal force, involving Newton’s laws and free-body analysis with tension at an angle and resistive force.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.3 Work, energy and power > A.3.2 Work and energy transfer
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.2 Work and energy transfer
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Final speed after pulling through a distance against a constant resistive force can be solved via work done by net force (work–energy theorem) using the horizontal component of tension.

parts.content: Determine the magnitude of the normal contact force exerted by the floor on the crate.
parts.markscheme:
weight $= 24 \times 9.81 (= 235.4\text{ N})$ OR vertical component of tension $= 110 \times \sin(32^\circ) (= 58.3\text{ N})$; <marks> 1 mark </marks>

$R = 235.4 - 58.3 = 177(\text{N})$; <marks> 1 mark </marks>

Allow use of $$g = 10\text{ m s}^{-2}$$. Weight $= 240\text{ N}$, Answer $= 181.7(\text{N})$.

parts.content: Calculate the component of the tension in the strap that acts parallel to the warehouse floor.
parts.markscheme:
horizontal component $= 110 \times \cos(32^\circ) = 93.3(\text{N})$; <marks> 1 mark </marks>

Allow a bald correct answer.

parts.content: A constant resistive force of $$65\text{ N}$$ acts on the crate. The crate starts from rest and is pulled through a distance of $$5.0\text{ m}$$. Calculate the final velocity of the crate at the end of this distance.
parts.markscheme:
net force $= 93.3 - 65 (= 28.3\text{ N})$; <marks> 1 mark </marks>

acceleration $= \frac{28.3}{24} (= 1.18\text{ m s}^{-2})$; <marks> 1 mark </marks>

$v^2 = u^2 + 2as$ OR $v = \sqrt{2 \times 1.18 \times 5.0}$; <marks> 1 mark </marks>

$3.44(\text{m s}^{-1})$; <marks> 1 mark </marks>

***OR***

net force $= 93.3 - 65 (= 28.3\text{ N})$; <marks> 1 mark </marks>

Work done $= F_{net} \times d = 28.3 \times 5.0 (= 141.5\text{ J})$; <marks> 1 mark </marks>

$141.5 = \frac{1}{2} \times 24 \times v^2$; <marks> 1 mark </marks>

$3.44(\text{m s}^{-1})$; <marks> 1 mark </marks>

Allow ECF from part (b).

questions.specification:
A block of mass $$0.50\text{ kg}$$ is moving at a velocity of $$+2.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$$ on a frictionless horizontal surface. It collides with a second block of mass $$1.50\text{ kg}$$ which is initially at rest. The table provides data for the velocities of the blocks before and after the collision.

| Block | Mass / kg | Velocity before collision / $$\text{m s}^{-1}$$ | Velocity after collision / $$\text{m s}^{-1}$$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | 0.50 | +2.0 | -1.0 |
| 2 | 1.50 | 0.0 | $$v$$ |

What is the velocity $$v$$ of the $$1.50\text{ kg}$$ block immediately after the collision?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.2 Forces and momentum > A.2.3 Collisions and explosions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.3 Collisions and explosions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question describes a collision between two blocks and asks for the final velocity of one of them, which is the core subject of the Collisions and explosions sub-topic.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.2 Forces and momentum > A.2.2 Linear momentum and impulse
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.2 Linear momentum and impulse
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The solution to the problem requires the application of the principle of conservation of linear momentum.

questions.specification:
A block of mass $$2.0\text{ kg}$$ is initially at rest on a frictionless horizontal surface. The graph shows how the acceleration $$a$$ of the block varies with time $$t$$.

![Graph of acceleration versus time](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-physics-new-0860418b-1516-42a1-954a-712406845d15-1771929956947/5d0ae558-b9da-4585-832e-9f7639841636.jpeg)

What is the momentum of the block at $$t = 16\text{ s}$$?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.2 Forces and momentum > A.2.2 Linear momentum and impulse
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.2 Linear momentum and impulse
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question specifically asks for the momentum of the block, which is defined by the mass and velocity (p=mv) and relates to impulse as the change in momentum (the area under a force-time graph, or mass times the area under an acceleration-time graph).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.1 Kinematics > A.1.1 Describing motion quantitatively and qualitatively
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.1 Describing motion quantitatively and qualitatively
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: To solve the problem, one must quantitatively interpret an acceleration-time graph, specifically by calculating the area under the curve to find the change in velocity, which is a core skill in kinematics.

questions.specification: A tennis ball of mass $0.060\text{ kg}$ is travelling horizontally at $25\text{ m s}^{-1}$ when it is struck by a racket. It leaves the racket in the opposite direction at $35\text{ m s}^{-1}$. The ball is in contact with the racket for $0.040\text{ s}$. What is the magnitude of the average force exerted by the racket on the ball?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.2 Forces and momentum > A.2.2 Linear momentum and impulse
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.2 Linear momentum and impulse
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses change in momentum over contact time to find average force (impulse-momentum theorem) for a ball reversing direction.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.2 Forces and momentum > A.2.3 Collisions and explosions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.3 Collisions and explosions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Context is an impact between racket and ball, but the calculation focuses on impulse/average force rather than collision modeling or energy conservation.

questions.specification: Sand falls vertically at a constant rate of $$R$$ kg s$$^{-1}$$ onto a horizontal tray. The sand hits the tray with a speed $$v$$ and comes to rest immediately. What is the average force exerted by the falling sand on the tray?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.2 Forces and momentum > A.2.2 Linear momentum and impulse
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.2 Linear momentum and impulse
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question calculates the average force as the rate of change of momentum (F = Δp/Δt). Since mass is falling at a rate R = Δm/Δt and coming to rest (Δv = v), the force F = R * v. This is a direct application of linear momentum and impulse.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.2 Forces and momentum > A.2.1 Newton's Laws of Motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.1 Newton's Laws of Motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The relationship between force and the rate of change of momentum is the fundamental definition of Newton's Second Law of Motion.

questions.specification: A comet of mass $m$ is moving with velocity $v$ in deep space. It breaks into two pieces: one of mass $\frac{1}{3}m$ and the other $\frac{2}{3}m$. If the smaller piece moves away at $6v$ relative to the original direction, what is the velocity of the larger piece relative to the original direction?

labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.3 Collisions and explosions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The comet breaks into two fragments (an explosion/fragmentation) in deep space, so the key principle is conservation of linear momentum in an explosion scenario to find the other fragment’s velocity.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.2 Forces and momentum > A.2.2 Linear momentum and impulse
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.2 Linear momentum and impulse
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Solving requires applying linear momentum conservation (total momentum before = total momentum after) using masses and velocities along the original direction.

questions.specification: A $1000 \text{ kg}$ rocket in deep space expels $50 \text{ kg}$ of fuel at $400 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ relative to the rocket. What is the speed gained by the rocket as a result of this ejection?

labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.2 Linear momentum and impulse
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Rocket fuel ejection in deep space is analyzed via conservation of linear momentum/impulse (rocket gains speed due to expelled mass and relative exhaust velocity).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.2 Forces and momentum > A.2.3 Collisions and explosions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.3 Collisions and explosions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The event is effectively an 'explosion'/mass ejection separation where momentum is conserved between rocket and expelled fuel.

questions.specification:
This question is about investigating the relationship between the pressure and volume of a gas at a constant temperature. 

![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-physics-new-14db12a3-cd51-4ad9-b43e-5bd3e91cfc7e-1771929956947/87395d99-e67e-4808-9292-fd7f3de66eaa.jpeg)

A student uses a gas syringe connected to a digital pressure sensor to measure the pressure $P$ of a trapped mass of air as its volume $V$ is varied. The experiment is performed slowly to ensure the temperature of the gas remains constant.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic B - The particulate nature of matter > B.3 Gas laws > B.3.1 Kinetic theory and gas behavior
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.3.1 Kinetic theory and gas behavior
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses ideal gas law at constant temperature to show P ∝ 1/V (Boyle’s law relationship) and interprets pressure–volume behavior for a trapped gas.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic B - The particulate nature of matter > B.3 Gas laws > B.3.2 Microscopic basis for macroscopic behavior
labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.3.2 Microscopic basis for macroscopic behavior
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Includes experimental investigation/graphing of macroscopic gas behavior (P vs 1/V) and using the graph to infer pressure at a given volume, which can be linked to macroscopic behavior arising from particle model.

parts.content: Show that for an ideal gas at constant temperature, the pressure $P$ is directly proportional to the reciprocal of the volume, $\frac{1}{V}$.
parts.markscheme:
- State the ideal gas equation $PV = nRT$ or Boyle's Law $PV = \text{constant}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Explain that if $n$, $R$, and $T$ are constant, then $P = \frac{k}{V}$, where $k$ is a constant, which implies $P \propto \frac{1}{V}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

2 marks total

parts.content:
The table below shows the measured values of pressure $P$ for different volumes $V$ of the trapped air. (Uncertainties in measurement are not shown in the table.)

| $V / \mathrm{cm^3}$ | $P / \mathrm{kPa}$ | $\frac{1}{V} / \mathrm{cm^{-3}}$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $\infty$ | 0 | 0 |
| 30.0 | 80 | .......... |
| 20.0 | 120 | .......... |
| 15.0 | 160 | .......... |
| 10.0 | 240 | .......... |

Complete the last column of the table.
Plot a graph of $P$ against $\frac{1}{V}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Correctly labelled axes with units ($P$ on y-axis, $1/V$ on x-axis) <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Appropriate linear scales using more than half the grid <marks>1 mark</marks>

- All data points from the table plotted accurately <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Line of best fit drawn as a straight line passing through the origin <marks>1 mark</marks>

4 marks total

parts.content: Consider the situation where the volume of the gas is adjusted to $12.5 \, \mathrm{cm^3}$. Use information from your graph to determine the pressure of the gas at this volume.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the reciprocal volume: $\frac{1}{12.5} = 0.080 \, \mathrm{cm^{-3}}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Read the corresponding pressure from the graph: $P = 192 \, \mathrm{kPa}$ (Accept range $188 - 196 \, \mathrm{kPa}$ depending on the line of best fit) <marks>1 mark</marks>

2 marks total

parts.content: The digital pressure sensor has an absolute uncertainty of $\pm 6 \, \mathrm{kPa}$. Calculate the percentage uncertainty in the pressure value determined in part (d).
parts.markscheme:
- State the formula for percentage uncertainty: $\frac{\Delta P}{P} \times 100$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Substitute values: $\frac{6}{192} \times 100 = 3.1 \%$ (Accept values between $3.0 \%$ and $3.2 \%$ based on part d) <marks>1 mark</marks>

2 marks total

questions.specification: An acrobat of mass $65\text{ kg}$ falls vertically from a platform located $12\text{ m}$ above a safety mat. After making contact with the mat, the acrobat is brought to rest in a time interval of $0.40\text{ s}$. Assume air resistance is negligible during the fall.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.2 Forces and momentum > A.2.1 Newton's Laws of Motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.1 Newton's Laws of Motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) asks for acceleration direction during contact with the mat and part (b) asks for average resultant force while stopping—both rely on Newton’s laws (net force related to acceleration).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.2 Forces and momentum > A.2.2 Linear momentum and impulse
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.2 Linear momentum and impulse
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Stopping over a given time interval naturally involves impulse/average force via change in momentum; average resultant force can be found from Δp/Δt.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.1 Kinematics > A.1.2 Equations of motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.2 Equations of motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The impact speed just before contacting the mat is determined from the 12 m free fall using constant-acceleration kinematics equations.

parts.content: State the direction of the acceleration of the acrobat during the period they are in contact with the mat.
parts.markscheme: - upwards (opposite to the direction of the initial velocity) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the magnitude of the average resultant force acting on the acrobat while they are being brought to rest.
parts.markscheme:
- calculates the speed of the acrobat just before impact: $v = \sqrt{2gh} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 12} = 15.34\text{ m s}^{-1}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- calculates the change in momentum: $\Delta p = m\Delta v = 65 \times 15.34 = 997.1\text{ N s}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- calculates the average resultant force: $F = \frac{\Delta p}{\Delta t} = \frac{997.1}{0.40} = 2492.75\text{ N} \approx 2500\text{ N}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Accept 2490 N or 2500 N depending on rounding. Allow ECF from velocity calculation.*

questions.specification: A $20 \text{ kg}$ satellite initially at rest explodes into two pieces. One of the pieces has a mass of $4 \text{ kg}$ and travels at $25 \text{ m s}^{-1}$. How much energy is supplied by the explosion?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.2 Forces and momentum > A.2.3 Collisions and explosions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.3 Collisions and explosions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Satellite explosion into two pieces implies using conservation of momentum to find the other fragment’s speed, then relating the increase in kinetic energy to energy supplied by the explosion.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.3 Work, energy and power > A.3.2 Work and energy transfer
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.2 Work and energy transfer
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Asks for energy supplied, which is found via kinetic energy change (work/energy transfer) after applying momentum conservation.

options.markscheme: This is $\tfrac12 m_1 v_1^2$ only, ignoring the kinetic energy of the second fragment.
This is $\tfrac12 m_2 v_2^2$ only, using $v_2=6.25\text{ m s}^{-1}$ but ignoring the first fragment.

Conservation of momentum (initially at rest): $m_1 v_1+m_2 v_2=0\Rightarrow v_2=-(4/16)\times 25=-6.25\text{ m s}^{-1}$. Energy supplied equals total final kinetic energy: $\tfrac12\cdot 4\cdot 25^2+\tfrac12\cdot 16\cdot 6.25^2=1250+312.5=1562.5\text{ J}\approx 1.56\times 10^3\text{ J}\approx 1560\text{ J}$.
This would be a looser rounding of the correct value $1562.5\text{ J}$; $1560\text{ J}$ is the closest option.

questions.specification:
A prototype maglev train is undergoing speed tests on a straight, horizontal guideway. The train starts from rest and accelerates uniformly until it reaches a maximum operational speed of $45 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ in a time of $15 \text{ s}$.

![Train](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-economics-1a668961-11a5-4810-bdd0-25635e6cb51e-1771937587718/6195782f-61cd-41c5-8a7d-07187692283f.jpeg)

Upon reaching this speed, the train maintains it for a distance of $1350 \text{ m}$ before entering a braking zone. In the braking zone, the train decelerates uniformly to a complete stop over a distance of $675 \text{ m}$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.1 Kinematics > A.1.2 Equations of motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.2 Equations of motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses constant-acceleration (SUVAT) equations to find acceleration, time at constant speed (via distance=speed×time), and deceleration from stopping distance.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.1 Kinematics > A.1.1 Describing motion quantitatively and qualitatively
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.1 Describing motion quantitatively and qualitatively
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Requires interpreting and sketching a velocity–time graph for the motion and using it to determine average speed over the whole journey (area under v–t curve / total time).

parts.content: Show that the magnitude of the train's acceleration during the first $15 \text{ s}$ is $3.0 \text{ m s}^{-2}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Use of $a = \frac{v - u}{t}$ or equivalent 

- Substitution: $a = \frac{45 - 0}{15} = 3.0 \text{ m s}^{-2}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the time, in seconds, for which the train travels at its constant maximum speed.
parts.markscheme:
- Use of $t = \frac{s}{v}$ for the constant velocity phase 

- Substitution: $t = \frac{1350}{45} = 30 \text{ s}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Determine the magnitude of the constant deceleration of the train during the final $675 \text{ m}$ of its journey.
parts.markscheme:
- Use of $v^2 = u^2 + 2as$ 

- Correct substitution: $0^2 = 45^2 + 2 \times a \times 675$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Correct calculation of magnitude: $a = \frac{-2025}{1350} \implies |a| = 1.5 \text{ m s}^{-2}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
Sketch, on the axes provided below, a velocity-time graph for the entire journey of the maglev train from start to finish.

![Axes](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-economics-1a668961-11a5-4810-bdd0-25635e6cb51e-1771937587718/8d58fe9f-25ca-477a-b724-96c373c978c0.jpeg)
parts.markscheme:
- Three-stage graph: diagonal line from (0,0) to (15,45), horizontal line to $t = 45 \text{ s}$, and diagonal line to $t = 75 \text{ s}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Correct key values on axes: peak at 45, horizontal segment from $t=15$ to $t=45$, and intercept at $t=75$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>The time for the braking phase is $\Delta t = \frac{v-u}{a} = \frac{0-45}{-1.5} = 30 \text{ s}$, so the final time is $15 + 30 + 30 = 75 \text{ s}$.</note>

parts.content: Calculate the average speed of the train for the entire journey.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate total distance: $s_{total} = \left(\frac{1}{2} \times 45 \times 15\right) + 1350 + 675 = 337.5 + 1350 + 675 = 2362.5 \text{ m}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Calculate total time: $t_{total} = 15 + 30 + 30 = 75 \text{ s}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

- State final answer with units: $v_{avg} = \frac{2362.5}{75} = 31.5 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
A uniform rigid bar is subjected to multiple vertical forces acting within the same vertical plane. The weight of the bar is negligible compared to the applied forces.

In which of the following configurations is the bar in static equilibrium?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.4 Rigid body mechanics (HL only) > A.4.1 Torque and rotational motion (HL only)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.4.1 Torque and rotational motion (HL only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Static equilibrium of a rigid bar under multiple vertical forces requires zero net force and zero net torque (moments) about any point; this is fundamentally a torque/rotational equilibrium question.

options.markscheme: This configuration represents a couple. While the net force is zero ($F - F = 0$), there is a non-zero net torque, causing the bar to rotate.
In this case, the net force is not zero ($F + F - F = F$ upward), so the bar will undergo translational acceleration.
Although the net force is zero ($F + F - 2F = 0$), the downward force is not centered. This creates a net clockwise torque about the center of the bar, so it is not in rotational equilibrium.

Static equilibrium requires both the net force and the net torque to be zero. 
- Net Force: $F + F - 2F = 0$
- Net Torque: Taking moments about the center, $(F \times d) - (F \times d) + (2F \times 0) = 0$.
Both conditions are satisfied.

questions.specification:
A student investigates the relationship between the angle of an inclined plane and the acceleration of a trolley rolling down the plane. The trolley is released from rest, and its acceleration is measured using a motion sensor placed at the top of the ramp.

The results of the experiment are recorded in the table below:
$$
\begin{array}{|l|l|}
\hline \text { Angle of Incline }(\theta) & \text { Acceleration }(a)\left(\mathrm{ms}^{-2}\right) \\
\hline 10^{\circ} & 1.70 \\
\hline 20^{\circ} & 3.34 \\
\hline 30^{\circ} & 4.90 \\
\hline 40^{\circ} & 6.20 \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

The theoretical relationship between acceleration and the angle of incline is given by:

$$
a=g \sin \theta
$$

where $g=9.81 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.2 Forces and momentum > A.2.1 Newton's Laws of Motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.1 Newton's Laws of Motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses the inclined-plane model a = g sinθ, which comes from resolving weight component along the slope via Newton’s second law; also asks for reasons experimental a is lower (e.g., friction/air resistance), i.e., forces affecting acceleration.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.1 Kinematics > A.1.1 Describing motion quantitatively and qualitatively
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.1 Describing motion quantitatively and qualitatively
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Involves measuring and plotting acceleration data, comparing to a theoretical linear relationship, and interpreting a graph to judge consistency with a model (kinematics representation/analysis of motion).

parts.content: Using the equation $a=g \sin \theta$, calculate the theoretical acceleration for each angle in the table and add it as a third column.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate $\sin \theta$ for each angle: $\sin(10^{\circ})=0.1736$, $\sin(20^{\circ})=0.3420$, $\sin(30^{\circ})=0.5000$, $\sin(40^{\circ})=0.6428$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate theoretical $a$ ($a=g \sin \theta$):
  For $\theta=10^{\circ}$, $a=(9.81)(0.1736)=1.70 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$
  For $\theta=20^{\circ}$, $a=(9.81)(0.3420)=3.36 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$
  For $\theta=30^{\circ}$, $a=(9.81)(0.5000)=4.91 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$
  For $\theta=40^{\circ}$, $a=(9.81)(0.6428)=6.31 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Add results to table: <marks>A1</marks>

| Angle of Incline $(\theta)$ | Acceleration $(a)(\mathrm{ms}^{-2})$ | Theoretical Acceleration $(\mathrm{ms}^{-2})$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $10^{\circ}$ | 1.70 | 1.70 |
| $20^{\circ}$ | 3.34 | 3.36 |
| $30^{\circ}$ | 4.90 | 4.91 |
| $40^{\circ}$ | 6.20 | 6.31 |

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify the formula
steps.content:
To find the theoretical acceleration, we use the specific relationship provided in the problem description. We are given the acceleration due to gravity, $g$, and we need to substitute each angle $\theta$ into the formula.

$$a = g \sin \theta$$

This formula is derived from Newton's Second Law ($$F=ma$$) applied to a mass on an incline, which is a core concept in **Topic A.2: Forces and momentum**.

steps.title: Calculate for first two angles
steps.content:
We calculate the theoretical acceleration for $10^{\circ}$ and $20^{\circ}$ by substituting the values into the formula. 

**For $\theta = 10^{\circ}$:**
$$\begin{aligned} a &= 9.81 \times \sin(10^{\circ}) \\ a &\approx 9.81 \times 0.1736 \\ a &= 1.70 \text{ m s}^{-2} \end{aligned}$$

**For $\theta = 20^{\circ}$:**
$$\begin{aligned} a &= 9.81 \times \sin(20^{\circ}) \\ a &\approx 9.81 \times 0.3420 \\ a &= 3.36 \text{ m s}^{-2} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions:
1. Set angle mode to Degrees.
2. Enter 9.81 * sin(10).
3. Enter 9.81 * sin(20).

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [MODE], ensure 'DEGREE' is highlighted.
2. Press [2ND] [QUIT].
3. Type 9.81 * sin(10) and press [ENTER].
4. Type 9.81 * sin(20) and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions:
1. Click the wrench icon (Settings) and select 'Degrees'.
2. Type 9.81 * sin(10).
3. Type 9.81 * sin(20).

calculator.description: Calculate theoretical acceleration for 10 and 20 degrees. Ensure your calculator is in DEGREE mode.

steps.title: Calculate for remaining angles
steps.content:
Repeat the substitution for $30^{\circ}$ and $40^{\circ}$.

**For $\theta = 30^{\circ}$:**
$$\begin{aligned} a &= 9.81 \times \sin(30^{\circ}) \\ a &= 9.81 \times 0.5000 \\ a &= 4.91 \text{ m s}^{-2} \end{aligned}$$

**For $\theta = 40^{\circ}$:**
$$\begin{aligned} a &= 9.81 \times \sin(40^{\circ}) \\ a &\approx 9.81 \times 0.6428 \\ a &= 6.31 \text{ m s}^{-2} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions:
1. Type 9.81 * sin(30) and press [ENTER].
2. Type 9.81 * sin(40) and press [ENTER].

calculator.description: Calculate theoretical acceleration for 30 and 40 degrees.

steps.title: Update the data table
steps.content:
Finally, we compile our calculated values into a third column in the table. 

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|l|}
\hline \text { Angle } (\theta) & \text { Accel. } (a) (\text{ms}^{-2}) & \text { Theoretical } a (\text{ms}^{-2}) \\
\hline 10^{\circ} & 1.70 & 1.70 \\
\hline 20^{\circ} & 3.34 & 3.36 \\
\hline 30^{\circ} & 4.90 & 4.91 \\
\hline 40^{\circ} & 6.20 & 6.31 \\
\hline
\end{array}$$

Notice that the theoretical values are slightly higher than the measured values, likely due to factors like friction or air resistance which are ignored in the simple model.

steps.title: Identify the Theoretical Relationship
steps.content:
The question asks us to use the theoretical formula provided to calculate the acceleration for different angles. This relationship is part of the study of forces on inclined planes, which you can review in **Topic A.2 Forces and momentum**.

We are given the formula:
$$a = g \sin \theta$$

Where:
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m s}^{-2}$ (the acceleration due to gravity)
- $\theta$ is the angle of incline in degrees.

steps.title: Input Data into GDC
steps.content:
To calculate all values efficiently, we will use the List function on your Graphing Display Calculator (GDC). First, enter the experimental angles into the first list ($L_1$).

**Important:** Ensure your calculator is set to **Degree mode** since the angles are given in degrees ($10^\circ, 20^\circ, \dots$).

gdc.instructions: Go to the 'Statistics' or 'List' menu. In List 1, enter 10, 20, 30, 40. Check system settings to ensure Angle is set to 'Degree'.

ti84.instructions: Press [STAT] [1:Edit]. In L1, enter 10, 20, 30, 40. Press [MODE] and ensure 'DEGREE' is highlighted.

desmos.instructions: Create a list: L_1 = [10, 20, 30, 40]. Ensure the settings toggle (wrench icon) is set to 'Degrees'.

calculator.description: Entering angles into a list for bulk calculation.

steps.title: Calculate Using List Formula
steps.content:
Now, we define the second list ($L_2$) using the theoretical formula. By applying the formula to the entire list, we get all four values at once:

$$L_2 = 9.81 \times \sin(L_1)$$

Rounding to two decimal places (to match the experimental data's precision), we get:
- For $10^\circ$: $9.81 \times \sin(10^\circ) \approx 1.70 \text{ m s}^{-2}$
- For $20^\circ$: $9.81 \times \sin(20^\circ) \approx 3.36 \text{ m s}^{-2}$
- For $30^\circ$: $9.81 \times \sin(30^\circ) \approx 4.91 \text{ m s}^{-2}$
- For $40^\circ$: $9.81 \times \sin(40^\circ) \approx 6.31 \text{ m s}^{-2}$

gdc.instructions: Highlight the header of List 2. Enter the formula '9.81 * sin(List 1)' and press EXE.

ti84.instructions: In the [STAT] [Edit] screen, scroll up to highlight the header 'L2'. Type '9.81 * sin(L1)' and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: In a new line, type the calculation using the list name.

calculator.description: Calculate theoretical acceleration for all angles simultaneously.

steps.title: Construct the Final Table
steps.content:
Finally, we present the data in the requested table format. In an IB exam, showing the $\sin \theta$ intermediate values can earn you a method mark (**M1**), while the final calculated accelerations earn an accuracy mark (**A1**).

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}
\hline \text{Angle } (\theta) & \text{Acc. } (a) \text{ (exp.)} & \text{Theoretical Acc. } (\text{m s}^{-2}) \\
\hline 10^\circ & 1.70 & 1.70 \\
\hline 20^\circ & 3.34 & 3.36 \\
\hline 30^\circ & 4.90 & 4.91 \\
\hline 40^\circ & 6.20 & 6.31 \\
\hline
\end{array}$$

Notice that the experimental and theoretical values are very close, but they diverge slightly as the angle increases, which is typical in real-world physics experiments.

parts.content: Plot a graph of experimental acceleration $a$ ($y$-axis) against $\sin \theta$ ($x$-axis). Use the graph to determine whether the experimental results are consistent with the theoretical model.
parts.markscheme:
- Plot the graph of experimental acceleration $a$ ($y$-axis) against $\sin \theta$ ($x$-axis) <marks>M1</marks>

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/plots/32779a69-42e4-4136-b9e2-01ae104ce101.png)

- State that a straight line through the origin indicates that acceleration is proportional to $\sin \theta$ <marks>R1</marks>

- Conclude that the experimental results support the theoretical model within limits of experimental error, noting that experimental values are consistently slightly lower than theoretical values <marks>R1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify plot axes
steps.content:
To check the relationship $$a = g \sin \theta$$, we must linearize the equation. The question asks us to plot experimental acceleration $$a$$ on the $$y$$-axis against $$\sin \theta$$ on the $$x$$-axis. 

This maps to the linear form $$y = mx + c$$:
- $$y = a$$
- $$x = \sin \theta$$
- Gradient $$m = g$$
- $$y$$-intercept $$c = 0$$

highlights.text: Plot a graph of experimental acceleration $a$ ($y$-axis) against $\sin \theta$ ($x$-axis)

steps.title: Calculate x-axis values
steps.content:
We need to calculate the value of $$\sin \theta$$ for each angle given in the table. 

$$\begin{aligned} \sin(10^\circ) &\approx 0.17 \\ \sin(20^\circ) &\approx 0.34 \\ \sin(30^\circ) &= 0.50 \\ \sin(40^\circ) &\approx 0.64 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Ensure angle setting is Degrees. Enter the list of angles and apply the sine function.

ti84.instructions: Ensure mode is set to DEGREE. Calculate sin(angle) for each value.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to get the decimal values.

calculator.description: Calculate sine values for the angles

steps.title: Plot the data points
steps.content:
Plot the points ($$\sin \theta$$, $$a$$) on the graph grid:

- $$(0.17, 1.70)$$
- $$(0.34, 3.34)$$
- $$(0.50, 4.90)$$
- $$(0.64, 6.20)$$

Draw a straight line of best fit through these points. The line should pass through or very close to the origin $$(0,0)$$.

steps.title: Check for linearity
steps.content:
Observe the shape of the graph:
1. The plotted points form a straight line pattern.
2. The line of best fit passes through the origin $$(0,0)$$.

In physics, a straight line through the origin indicates that the variable on the $$y$$-axis is directly proportional to the variable on the $$x$$-axis ($$a \propto \sin \theta$$).

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.content:
Since the graph is a straight line passing through the origin, the experimental results are consistent with the theoretical model $$a = g \sin \theta$$. 

However, note that the experimental points may lie slightly below the theoretical line (since $$g \approx 9.8$$ and $$6.20 < 9.8 \times 0.64 \approx 6.27$$), which could be due to friction or air resistance.

steps.title: Linearize the equation
steps.content:
To perform the **Gradient Comparison**, we first need to express the theoretical relationship as a linear equation of the form $y = mx + c$.

The given equation is:
$$a = g \sin \theta$$

Comparing this to $y = mx + c$:
- $y = a$ (acceleration)
- $x = \sin \theta$
- $m = g$ (the gradient represents the acceleration due to gravity)
- $c = 0$ (the line should pass through the origin)

Therefore, if we plot $a$ against $\sin \theta$, we expect a straight line with a gradient equal to $g$.

steps.title: Calculate x-axis data
steps.content:
We need to calculate the values for the x-axis ($x = \sin \theta$) corresponding to each angle in the table.

$$\begin{aligned} \sin(10^{\circ}) &\approx 0.174 \\ \sin(20^{\circ}) &\approx 0.342 \\ \sin(30^{\circ}) &= 0.500 \\ \sin(40^{\circ}) &\approx 0.643 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: In Run-Matrix mode, set Angle unit to Degrees. Enter sin(10), etc.

ti84.instructions: Ensure mode is set to DEGREES. Calculate sin(10), sin(20), sin(30), sin(40).

desmos.instructions: Type sin(10 deg) to calculate values.

calculator.description: Calculate sin(theta) for each angle

steps.title: Plot the graph
steps.content:
Now, plot the data points ($x, y$) on the graph:
- $(0.174, 1.70)$
- $(0.342, 3.34)$
- $(0.500, 4.90)$
- $(0.643, 6.20)$

Draw a **line of best fit** through these points. Since the theoretical equation has no y-intercept ($c=0$), the line should pass through or very close to the origin $(0,0)$.

highlights.text: Plot a graph of experimental acceleration a (y-axis) against sin θ (x-axis)

steps.title: Calculate the gradient
steps.content:
Select two points on your line of best fit to calculate the gradient ($m$). For accuracy, choose points far apart, for example, the origin $(0,0)$ and the point for $30^{\circ}$ which lies almost perfectly on the trend $(0.50, 4.90)$.

$$m = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \frac{4.90 - 0}{0.500 - 0} = 9.80 \text{ m s}^{-2}$$

ti84.instructions: Calculate the slope using division.

calculator.description: Verify gradient calculation

steps.title: Compare with theory
steps.content:
The theoretical value for the gradient is $g = 9.81 \text{ m s}^{-2}$.

Our experimental gradient is approximately $9.80 \text{ m s}^{-2}$ (or slightly lower depending on your exact line of best fit, e.g., using all points might yield $\approx 9.73$).

The graph is a straight line through the origin, confirming that $a \propto \sin \theta$. The experimental gradient is very close to the theoretical value of $g$, supporting the model. The slight difference (experimental values being slightly lower) is likely due to friction, which opposes motion and reduces acceleration.

parts.content: The experimental accelerations are found to be slightly lower than the theoretical values. Suggest two reasons for this discrepancy and explain how the experimental setup could be improved to reduce these errors.
parts.markscheme:
- State that friction between the trolley and the inclined plane reduces the observed acceleration <marks>A1</marks>
- Suggest using a smoother ramp or applying lubrication to minimize friction <marks>A1</marks>

- State that air resistance acting on the trolley may also reduce its acceleration slightly <marks>A1</marks>
- Suggest using a heavier trolley to reduce the relative impact of air resistance <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

<note>Award 1 mark for each relevant reason and 1 mark for the explanation on the improvement.</note>

steps.title: Verify the discrepancy
steps.content:
First, let's verify the statement that experimental values are lower than theoretical ones. Using the given formula for $\theta = 40^{\circ}$:

$$
a_{\text{theoretical}} = 9.81 \times \sin(40^{\circ}) \approx 6.31 \text{ m s}^{-2}
$$

The experimental value in the table is $6.20 \text{ m s}^{-2}$. Since $6.20 < 6.31$, there is indeed a resistive force opposing the motion that the theoretical model ignores. We need to identify two such forces.

ti84.instructions: Enter 9.81 * sin(40). Ensure mode is set to DEGREES.

calculator.description: Calculate theoretical acceleration for 40 degrees

highlights.text: The experimental accelerations are found to be slightly lower than the theoretical values.

steps.title: Reason 1: Friction
steps.content:
The theoretical model assumes the contact surfaces are perfectly smooth. In reality, **friction** acts between the wheels of the trolley and the ramp (and within the wheel bearings).

Friction is a non-conservative force that acts in the direction opposite to the motion. This reduces the net force acting down the slope:

$$
F_{\text{net}} = mg \sin \theta - F_{\text{friction}}
$$

Consequently, the measured acceleration is less than $g \sin \theta$.

steps.title: Improvement 1: Reducing friction
steps.content:
To reduce the error caused by friction, we need to minimize the contact resistance.

**Improvement:** Use a **smoother ramp** surface or apply **lubrication** (oil/grease) to the wheel axles and the ramp surface. This reduces the coefficient of friction, bringing the experimental conditions closer to the frictionless theoretical model.

steps.title: Reason 2: Air resistance
steps.content:
The second non-conservative force is **air resistance** (or drag). As the trolley accelerates down the ramp, it moves through the air, creating a resistive force opposite to its velocity.

The theoretical equation $a=g \sin \theta$ ignores this fluid resistance. Even though the trolley is not moving very fast, this force still contributes to a slight reduction in the net force and acceleration.

steps.title: Improvement 2: Minimizing air resistance impact
steps.content:
We cannot remove the air, but we can reduce the *effect* of air resistance on the acceleration.

**Improvement:** Use a **heavier (more massive) trolley**.

Consider Newton's Second Law with drag included:

$$
a = \frac{F_{\text{net}}}{m} = \frac{mg \sin \theta - F_{\text{drag}}}{m} = g \sin \theta - \frac{F_{\text{drag}}}{m}
$$

By increasing the mass $m$, the term $\frac{F_{\text{drag}}}{m}$ becomes smaller, making the observed acceleration $a$ closer to the theoretical value $g \sin \theta$.

questions.specification:
A uniform rigid beam of mass 15.0 kg and length 3.00 m is hinged to a vertical wall. It is held in a horizontal position by a light cable attached to the right end of the beam and to a point on the wall directly above the hinge. The cable makes an angle of 35.0° with the horizontal beam.

![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-physics-new-22d49a4c-5529-462f-9608-d0f3063da8bb-1771929956947/aeb2871a-09e8-4082-8e81-f83dc503ac32.jpeg)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.4 Rigid body mechanics (HL only) > A.4.1 Torque and rotational motion (HL only)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.4.1 Torque and rotational motion (HL only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The beam held by a cable and hinge in static equilibrium requires taking moments (torque balance) about the hinge to find the cable tension and assess changes when an extra mass is added; also involves reasoning about hinge reaction forces.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.2 Forces and momentum > A.2.1 Newton's Laws of Motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.1 Newton's Laws of Motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) explicitly asks about vertical force balance for equilibrium (net force = 0) and hinge reaction forces, which uses Newton’s laws in static situations alongside the torque analysis.

parts.content: Outline why the hinge must exert a vertical upward force on the beam for it to remain in equilibrium.
parts.markscheme:
The vertical component of the cable tension ($T \sin 35.0^\circ$) is less than the total weight of the beam (acting downwards)  <marks>1 mark</marks>

Therefore, for translational equilibrium ($ \sum F_y = 0 $), the hinge must provide the remaining upward vertical force.

parts.content: Calculate the tension $T$ in the cable.
parts.markscheme:
Taking moments about the hinge: 
Clockwise moments = Anticlockwise moments

$(15.0 \times 9.81) \times 1.50 = (T \sin 35.0^\circ) \times 3.00$  <marks>1 mark</marks>

$220.725 = 1.7207 \times T$

$T = 128$ N  <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Allow 130 N if $g=10$ is used.</note>

parts.content: A mass of 8.0 kg is suspended from the right end of the beam. The cable has a maximum breaking strength of 250 N. Determine whether the cable will snap.
parts.markscheme:
New total clockwise moment = $(15.0 \times 9.81 \times 1.50) + (8.0 \times 9.81 \times 3.00) = 456.165$ Nm

$T_{\text{new}} = \frac{456.165}{3.00 \sin 35.0^\circ} = 265$ N  <marks>1 mark</marks>

Since $265$ N $> 250$ N, the cable will snap.  <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
A ball is kicked horizontally off the edge of a vertical cliff of height $h$ with an initial horizontal velocity $v_x$, as shown in the diagram. The ball follows a parabolic trajectory and lands on a horizontal surface at a distance $D$ from the base of the cliff. Air resistance is negligible.

![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-chemistry-new-2832728b-71b4-439c-8f91-82dad1dc7a87-1771938518510/e77e99d5-eed5-4439-926c-1b9d382b856a.jpeg)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.1 Kinematics > A.1.3 Projectile motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.3 Projectile motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The scenario is a horizontal launch from a cliff with negligible air resistance, requiring separation of horizontal/vertical motion, time of flight from vertical free-fall, range calculation, and velocity components—core projectile motion skills.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.1 Kinematics > A.1.2 Equations of motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.2 Equations of motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (b) and (c) explicitly use constant-acceleration kinematic equations in the vertical direction (e.g., h = 1/2 g t^2) to derive and compute time of flight.

parts.content: State the initial value of the vertical component of the ball's velocity.
parts.markscheme: Zero / $0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Derive an expression for the time $t$ the ball takes to reach the ground in terms of $h$ and $g$.
parts.markscheme:
Consider vertical motion with $u_y = 0$:

$$h = u_y t + \frac{1}{2}gt^2 \implies h = \frac{1}{2}gt^2$$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

Solving for $t$:

$$t = \sqrt{\frac{2h}{g}}$$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the time of flight for a ball projected from a cliff with a height $h = 32 \text{ m}$. Take $g = 9.8 \text{ m s}^{-2}$.
parts.markscheme:
$$t = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 32}{9.8}}$$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

$$t = 2.55 \text{ s}$$ (accept $2.6 \text{ s}$ or $2.56 \text{ s}$) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Draw a vector diagram showing the velocity of the ball and its horizontal and vertical components at the instant it is halfway (in terms of time) through its flight. Label the resultant velocity $v$, the horizontal component $v_x$, and the vertical component $v_y$.
parts.markscheme:
Diagram showing:
- Horizontal vector $v_x$ pointing right <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Vertical vector $v_y$ pointing downward <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Resultant vector $v$ acting at an angle between $v_x$ and $v_y$ forming a vector triangle or rectangle <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Length of $v_x$ should be constant; $v_y$ should be approximately half its final impact length.</note>

parts.content: Determine an expression for the initial horizontal speed $v_x$ in terms of $D$, $h$, and $g$.
parts.markscheme:
Horizontal distance $D = v_x t$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

Substitute expression for $t$ from part (b):

$$D = v_x \sqrt{\frac{2h}{g}}$$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

Rearrange for $v_x$:

$$v_x = D \sqrt{\frac{g}{2h}}$$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe the motion of the cork based on the shape of the graph.
parts.markscheme:
- The graph gradient is positive and decreasing (steepest at start) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This indicates the cork is moving upwards with decreasing speed (decelerating) <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Relate gradient to velocity
steps.content:
To describe the motion using a height-time ($h-t$) graph, we first recognize that the gradient (slope) of the curve represents the instantaneous velocity of the cork. This is a fundamental concept from **Topic A.1: Kinematics**.

$$v = \frac{\Delta h}{\Delta t}$$

highlights.text: The graph shows how the vertical height of the cork changes with time.

steps.title: Identify the direction
steps.content: By looking at the graph, we can see that as time increases, the height also increases. This indicates that the gradient is positive, meaning the cork is moving in the upward direction.

steps.title: Analyze the steepness
steps.content:
Next, we examine how the steepness of the curve changes over time. 

At $t=0$, the curve is at its steepest. As time progresses, the curve becomes flatter (less steep). A decreasing gradient on an $h-t$ graph tells us that the magnitude of the velocity is decreasing over time.

steps.title: State the final motion
steps.content:
Combining these observations, we can conclude that the cork is moving **upwards with decreasing speed** (or decelerating). 

According to the markscheme, you earn marks for:
1. Identifying that the **gradient is positive and decreasing**.
2. Stating that the cork moves **upwards with decreasing speed**.

highlights.text: Describe the motion of the cork based on the shape of the graph.

steps.title: Identify general motion direction
steps.content:
To describe the motion, we first look at the overall trend of the graph. Since the vertical height increases as time passes, we can conclude that the cork is moving upwards. This aligns with the context of a cork being launched from a bottle.

This is a qualitative analysis of **Topic A.1 Kinematics**, specifically describing motion through graphical representation.

highlights.text: vertical height of the cork changes with time

steps.title: Analyze equal time intervals
steps.content:
To understand how the speed changes, let's use **interval analysis**. We compare the vertical displacement (change in height) over equal time intervals. 

Looking at the x-axis (time), let's compare the first $0.1 \text{ s}$ to the next $0.1 \text{ s}$:
- From $t = 0.0$ to $0.1 \text{ s}$, the height increases by approximately $1.4 \text{ m}$.
- From $t = 0.1$ to $0.2 \text{ s}$, the height increases by approximately $1.0 \text{ m}$.

Since the vertical distance covered in the second interval is less than the first, the displacement per unit time is reducing.

steps.title: Relate displacement to velocity
steps.content:
Recall the fundamental definition of average velocity:

$$v_{avg} = \frac{\Delta s}{\Delta t}$$

Because the time interval ($\Delta t$) remains constant while the displacement ($\Delta s$) decreases, the velocity must be decreasing. On a displacement-time graph, the velocity is represented by the gradient (slope). Therefore, the shape of the graph shows a **positive but decreasing gradient**.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
Based on our interval analysis, we can summarize the motion for the two marks:
1. The graph's gradient is positive and decreasing.
2. This indicates the cork is moving **upwards with decreasing speed** (or decelerating).

This deceleration is expected because gravity acts downwards on the cork after it has been launched.

highlights.text: Describe the motion of the cork based on the shape of the graph

steps.title: Analyze the graph gradient
steps.content:
To describe the motion, we first look at the gradient (slope) of the vertical height–time graph. In kinematics, the gradient of a displacement-time graph represents the velocity of the object.

$$\text{velocity} = \frac{\Delta \text{displacement}}{\Delta \text{time}}$$

Looking at the graph, the line starts very steep and gradually levels out. This means the **gradient is positive but decreasing** over time.

highlights.text: vertical height of the cork changes with time

steps.title: Interpret the velocity
steps.content:
Because the gradient is positive, we know the cork is moving in the positive vertical direction (upwards). Since the gradient (velocity) is getting smaller (less steep) as time passes, the cork's speed is decreasing.

This pattern—a parabolic curve where the slope decreases—is the characteristic "signature" of an object moving against a constant force like gravity.

steps.title: Identify the acceleration
steps.content:
A decreasing velocity indicates that the cork is **decelerating**. 

In IB Physics, we recognize this specific parabolic shape from **Topic A.1 (Kinematics)** as motion under constant acceleration. Since the cork is moving up and slowing down, it is undergoing a constant negative acceleration (downward), which we would expect since air resistance is neglected and only gravity is acting on it.

steps.title: Final Description
steps.content:
To earn full marks, your description should link the visual evidence to the physical motion:

1. The gradient of the graph is positive and decreasing.
2. This indicates the cork is moving **upwards** and **decelerating** (or moving with decreasing speed).

parts.content: Estimate the initial vertical speed of the cork.
parts.markscheme:
- Draw tangent at $t=0$ and calculate gradient, e.g. using points $(0,0)$ and $(0.02, 0.12)$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Estimated speed: $v \approx 6.0 \mathrm{~m\,s^{-1}}$ (accept $5.5 - 6.5$) <marks>1 mark</marks>

2 marks total

parts.content: Assuming the cork is accelerated uniformly over a distance of $0.015 \mathrm{~m}$, use your estimated launch speed to determine the net force acting on the cork.
parts.markscheme:
- Use of kinematic equation $v^{2}=u^{2}+2as$ (or $v^{2}=2ad$) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Correct substitution: $a=\frac{5.9^{2}}{2 \times 0.015} \approx 1160 \mathrm{~m\,s^{-2}}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Calculate net force: $F=ma=0.008 \times 1160 = 9.3 \mathrm{~N}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

3 marks total

parts.content: Estimate the pressure inside the bottle using your result for the force. Give your answer with units.
parts.markscheme: - Pressure $P=\frac{F}{A}=\frac{9.3}{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}=3.7 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{~Pa}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.markscheme: - Assumption: force is constant over the distance; cork receives all pressure uniformly over its area <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
A student uses an electronic timer in an attempt to estimate the acceleration of free-fall $g$.
She measures the time $t$ taken for a small metal ball to fall through a height $h$ of 0.50 m.
The percentage uncertainty in the measurement of time is 0.3% and the percentage uncertainty in height is 0.6%.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.1 Kinematics > A.1.2 Equations of motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.2 Equations of motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses the constant-acceleration equation h = (1/2) g t^2 for free-fall and requires rearranging for g; also part (b) concerns improving an experimental determination of g based on this kinematics relation.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.1 Kinematics > A.1.1 Describing motion quantitatively and qualitatively
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.1 Describing motion quantitatively and qualitatively
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Involves measurement uncertainties/percentage uncertainty propagation in an experimental estimate of g from measured h and t, which is commonly assessed alongside quantitative description of motion.

parts.content: Using $h=\frac{1}{2} g t^2$ calculate the expected percentage uncertainty in the value of $g$.
parts.markscheme: the estimated percentage uncertainty in $g$ is $2\times 0.3 + 0.6 = 1.2\%$ <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Rearrange the equation
steps.content:
To find the uncertainty in $g$, we first rearrange the given formula to make $g$ the subject.

Starting with:
$$h = \frac{1}{2}gt^2$$

Multiply both sides by 2:
$$2h = gt^2$$

Divide by $t^2$:
$$g = \frac{2h}{t^2}$$

steps.title: Identify percentage uncertainties
steps.content:
Next, we identify the percentage uncertainty values provided in the question for height ($h$) and time ($t$).

*   Percentage uncertainty in height ($h$): $0.6\%$
*   Percentage uncertainty in time ($t$): $0.3\%$

highlights.text: percentage uncertainty in the measurement of time is 0.3%

steps.title: Apply propagation rules
steps.content:
We apply the standard uncertainty propagation rules for multiplication, division, and powers:

1.  **Constants:** The number $2$ is an exact constant and has no uncertainty.
2.  **Powers:** For $t^2$, we multiply the percentage uncertainty of $t$ by the power $2$.
3.  **Summation:** We add the percentage uncertainties of the variables $h$ and $t^2$.

This gives us the equation:
$$\% \Delta g = \% \Delta h + 2(\% \Delta t)$$

steps.title: Calculate the final result
steps.content:
Substitute the values into our propagation equation:

$$\begin{aligned} \% \Delta g &= 0.6\% + 2(0.3\%) \\ \% \Delta g &= 0.6\% + 0.6\% \\ \% \Delta g &= 1.2\% \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the sum of the uncertainties:

ti84.instructions: Calculate the sum of the uncertainties:

desmos.instructions: Calculate the sum of the uncertainties:

calculator.description: Calculate the total percentage uncertainty

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content: The expected percentage uncertainty in the value of $g$ is **$1.2\%$**.

parts.content: State and explain how the student could obtain a more reliable value for $g$.
parts.markscheme:
- use more than one height <marks> 1 mark </marks>
- obtain $g$ from a suitable graph (of height $h$ versus $t^2$) <marks> 1 mark </marks>
- $g$ is twice the gradient <marks> 1 mark </marks>

***OR***

- use a denser ball (to reduce air resistance) <marks> 1 mark </marks>
- use a (much) larger height <marks> 1 mark </marks>
- repeat many times (to get an average of time) <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Vary the height
steps.content: To improve the reliability of the result, the student should measure the time of fall $t$ for **several different heights** $h$, rather than relying on a single measurement at 0.50 m. This produces a set of data points that can be analyzed together.

steps.title: Identify the linear relationship
steps.content:
Using the kinematic equation for displacement from the data booklet, where initial velocity $u=0$, acceleration $a=g$, and displacement $s=h$:

$$s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2 \implies h = \frac{1}{2}gt^2$$

This equation suggests a linear relationship between height $h$ and time squared $t^2$.

steps.title: Plot a graph
steps.content: The student should plot a graph of **height $h$ on the vertical axis** versus **time squared $t^2$ on the horizontal axis**. Drawing a line of best fit through these points helps average out random errors in the individual timing measurements.

steps.title: Calculate g from the gradient
steps.content:
By comparing the experimental equation $h = \frac{1}{2}gt^2$ with the straight line equation $y = mx$, we can see that the gradient (slope) $m$ represents half of $g$.

$$\text{gradient} = \frac{g}{2}$$

Therefore, the student can calculate a more reliable value for $g$ by doubling the gradient of the line of best fit:

$$g = 2 \times \text{gradient}$$

steps.title: Analyze the experimental setup
steps.content: To obtain a more reliable value for $g$, we need to consider how to reduce uncertainties and errors in the experiment. The question states the student currently uses a single height of $0.50\text{ m}$ and measures the time once. We can improve this procedure using **Experimental Refinements** targeting random errors, percentage uncertainties, and systematic errors.

steps.title: Reduce random error by repeating
steps.content:
The first refinement is to **repeat the measurement** of time $t$ multiple times for the same height. 

By calculating the mean (average) value of $t$, you minimize the impact of random errors in the timing process, leading to a more reliable result.

steps.title: Reduce percentage uncertainty with height
steps.content:
The second refinement is to use a **much larger height** than $0.50\text{ m}$.

Using the kinematic equation from the data booklet:
$$s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2$$

Since $u=0$ and $a=g$, we have $h = \frac{1}{2}gt^2$. A larger height $h$ results in a longer fall time $t$. Since the absolute uncertainty in the timer is fixed (e.g., $\pm 0.01\text{ s}$), measuring a longer time decreases the **percentage uncertainty** in $t$.

steps.title: Minimize air resistance
steps.content:
The third refinement is to use a **denser ball** (like a steel ball bearing) or an object with a smaller surface area-to-mass ratio.

This reduces the effect of **air resistance** relative to the weight of the ball, ensuring the acceleration is closer to the true free-fall value $g$ rather than being reduced by drag forces.

![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-economics-36ac1dd7-f503-44d9-be7e-794cef0ee52e-1771937587718/4974c3d1-523c-43de-bb65-edbfd98fc994.jpeg)

What is the speed of the object at $t = 6.0\text{ s}$?

options.markscheme: <note>This incorrect value is obtained by dividing the maximum force (8 N) by the mass (2 kg).</note>
<note>This incorrect value is obtained by calculating only half of the area under the graph (from 0 to 3 s) and dividing by the mass.</note>

<note>
- The impulse is the area under the force-time graph: $A = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height} = \frac{1}{2} \times 6.0\text{ s} \times 8.0\text{ N} = 24\text{ N s}$.
- Using the impulse-momentum theorem: $\Delta p = F\Delta t = m\Delta v$.
- $24\text{ N s} = 2.0\text{ kg} \times (v - 0)$.
- Therefore, $v = 12\text{ m s}^{-1}$.
</note>
<note>This incorrect value is obtained if the student forgets the $\frac{1}{2}$ factor in the triangle area calculation ($6 \times 8 / 2$).</note>

questions.specification:
A mass $m$ is suspended by two ropes. The tensions in the two ropes are $T_1$ and $T_2$. The angles between the ropes and the ceiling are $72^\circ$ and $28^\circ$ respectively. The system is in equilibrium.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/GDeW5_9ngLVJeFCR8jueA.jpeg)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.2 Forces and momentum > A.2.1 Newton's Laws of Motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.1 Newton's Laws of Motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question is entirely about static equilibrium of a mass suspended by two ropes: conditions for equilibrium (net force zero), resolving tension components, free-body diagram, and calculating tensions from force balance—direct application of Newton’s laws.

parts.content: State the two conditions necessary for the load to remain in equilibrium.
parts.markscheme:
- State the condition for forces: The resultant force on the load must be zero <marks>A1</marks>
- State the condition for torque: The resultant torque about any point must be zero (or net moment = 0) <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Define Static Equilibrium
steps.content:
To solve this, we need to recall what it means for an object to be in **static equilibrium**. In the IB curriculum, this is covered in **Topic A.2 (Forces)** and **Topic A.4 (Rigid Body Mechanics)**. 

For an object like this suspended mass to remain completely still, it must satisfy two distinct physical requirements: one relating to its straight-line motion (translation) and one relating to its rotation.

highlights.text: State the two conditions necessary for the load to remain in equilibrium.

steps.title: Requirement 1: Translational Equilibrium
steps.content:
The first condition is that the object must not accelerate in any direction. According to Newton's Second Law ($$F = ma$$), if the acceleration is zero, the total force must be zero.

**Condition 1:** The resultant (or net) force acting on the object must be zero.

$$\sum \vec{F} = 0$$

highlights.text: The resultant force on the load must be zero

steps.title: Requirement 2: Rotational Equilibrium
steps.content:
The second condition ensures that the object does not start rotating. This means that all the "turning effects" (moments) must balance each other out around any chosen point.

**Condition 2:** The resultant (or net) torque (or moment) about any point must be zero.

$$\sum \vec{\tau} = 0$$

highlights.text: The resultant torque about any point must be zero (or net moment = 0)

parts.content: Outline why the magnitudes of $T_1$ and $T_2$ are different.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify horizontal equilibrium condition: $T_1 \cos(72^\circ) = T_2 \cos(28^\circ)$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Reason that since $\cos(72^\circ) \neq \cos(28^\circ)$, the tensions $T_1$ and $T_2$ must differ in magnitude to balance the horizontal forces <marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify horizontal equilibrium condition
steps.content:
Since the system is in a state of equilibrium, the net force acting on the mass must be zero in all directions. In the horizontal direction, this means the leftward component of $T_1$ must exactly balance the rightward component of $T_2$.

Using trigonometry, we resolve each tension into its horizontal component:

$$T_1 \cos(72^\circ) = T_2 \cos(28^\circ)$$ 

This equation ensures there is no horizontal acceleration.

steps.title: Compare the trigonometric factors
steps.content: To see why the tensions are different, we can calculate the values of the cosine factors. For the horizontal forces to balance, the product of the tension and the cosine of its angle must be equal for both ropes.

gdc.instructions: Set angle units to degrees. Calculate cos(72) and cos(28).

ti84.instructions: Ensure the calculator is in DEGREE mode. Press [COS] 72 [ENTER] and [COS] 28 [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Set the graph settings to Degrees. Type cos(72) and cos(28) into separate lines.

calculator.description: Calculate the cosine values for the two angles.

steps.title: Reason the tension difference
steps.content:
From our calculations:
$$\cos(72^\circ) \approx 0.309$$
$$\cos(28^\circ) \approx 0.883$$

Because $\cos(72^\circ) \neq \cos(28^\circ)$, the magnitudes $T_1$ and $T_2$ must be different to satisfy the equilibrium equation. Specifically, since $\cos(72^\circ)$ is much smaller than $\cos(28^\circ)$, $T_1$ must be significantly larger than $T_2$ to maintain the horizontal balance.

steps.title: Establish the Closed Triangle
steps.content: Since the system is in equilibrium, the net force acting on the mass is zero. In physics, this means that the three force vectors—tension $T_1$, tension $T_2$, and the weight $W$—must form a closed vector triangle when placed tip-to-tail.

steps.title: Analyze Horizontal Balance
steps.content:
For the triangle to close horizontally, the horizontal component of $T_1$ must exactly balance the horizontal component of $T_2$. 

Using the angles provided relative to the ceiling:
$$T_1 \cos(72^\circ) = T_2 \cos(28^\circ)$$ 

This is the mathematical condition for horizontal equilibrium.

steps.title: Compare Geometric Components
steps.content:
Now we compare the trigonometric values:
- $\cos(72^\circ) \approx 0.31$
- $\cos(28^\circ) \approx 0.88$

Since these angles are different, their cosines are unequal. In the vector triangle, this corresponds to the sides having different 'tilts' or inclinations. To maintain the same horizontal 'width' (the balance of forces), the lengths of the sides representing $T_1$ and $T_2$ must be different.

gdc.instructions: Set angle units to Degrees. Calculate cos(72) and cos(28).

ti84.instructions: Ensure the calculator is in Degree mode. Press [COS] 72 [ENTER] and [COS] 28 [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Switch to Degree mode in settings. Type cos(72) and cos(28).

calculator.description: Evaluate the cosines of the given angles to show they are unequal.

steps.title: State the Geometric Conclusion
steps.content: In a triangle, if the interior angles are unequal, the side lengths opposite those angles must also be unequal. Because $\cos(72^\circ) \neq \cos(28^\circ)$, the magnitudes of $T_1$ and $T_2$ must be different to satisfy the equilibrium condition.

parts.content: Draw a free-body diagram showing the forces acting on the load.
parts.markscheme:
- Correct forces drawn: Tension $T_1$, Tension $T_2$ (inclined), and Weight ($mg$) vertically downward <marks>A1</marks>
- Forces labelled with correct directions <marks>A1</marks>

![Free Body Diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/a1007ee0-199b-40f7-a8bb-8dc37d2aa6a8)

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the acting forces
steps.content:
To draw a free-body diagram (FBD), we first identify every individual force acting on the mass $m$. 

There are three forces at play here:
1.  **Weight ($W$ or $mg$):** The gravitational pull acting vertically downwards.
2.  **Tension $T_1$:** The pull from the first rope acting along the rope toward the left ceiling attachment.
3.  **Tension $T_2$:** The pull from the second rope acting along the rope toward the right ceiling attachment.

steps.title: Represent the point mass
steps.content: In a standard free-body diagram, we represent the object (the load) as a single point. This simplifies the visualization of how forces act on the center of mass. All force vectors should originate from this point and point away from it.

steps.title: Draw the weight vector
steps.content:
Start by drawing the weight vector. Since gravity always acts toward the center of the Earth, draw a straight arrow pointing vertically downwards from your center point. Label this arrow as $W$, $mg$, or **Weight**.

From the data booklet, we know force is related to mass and acceleration:

$$F = ma$$

In this case, the force of gravity is $$W = mg$$.

steps.title: Draw the tension vectors
steps.content:
Next, draw the two tension forces acting away from the point mass along the directions of the ropes:

*   **Tension $T_1$:** Draw an arrow pointing up and to the left. The angle between this vector and a horizontal line (representing the ceiling) is $72^\circ$.
*   **Tension $T_2$:** Draw an arrow pointing up and to the right. The angle between this vector and the horizontal line is $28^\circ$.

Ensure the labels $T_1$ and $T_2$ are clearly visible next to their respective arrows.

steps.title: Finalize and check diagram
steps.content:
Review your diagram to ensure it meets the markscheme criteria:
1.  **Three forces** are present: $T_1$, $T_2$, and $mg$.
2.  **Directions** are correct: $mg$ is vertical; $T_1$ and $T_2$ are inclined upwards at the specified angles.
3.  **Labels** are clear.

Since the system is in **equilibrium**, the vector sum of these three forces must be zero, meaning the upward components of the tensions balance the weight, and their horizontal components cancel each other out.

highlights.text: Tension $T_1$, Tension $T_2$ (inclined), and Weight ($mg$) vertically downward

parts.content: Calculate the ratio $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2}$ based on the angles given.
parts.markscheme:
- State the horizontal equilibrium equation: $T_1 \cos(72^\circ) = T_2 \cos(28^\circ)$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Rearrange to find the ratio: $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = \dfrac{\cos(28^\circ)}{\cos(72^\circ)}$
- Calculate the final value: $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = \dfrac{0.8829}{0.3090} \approx 2.86$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze horizontal equilibrium
steps.content:
Since the system is in equilibrium, the net force in every direction must be zero. For the horizontal direction, this means the leftward horizontal component of $T_1$ must exactly balance the rightward horizontal component of $T_2$.

This concept is a direct application of Newton's First Law, which you can review in **Topic A.2: Forces and momentum**.

steps.title: Establish the equilibrium equation
steps.content:
Using the angles given between the ropes and the ceiling, we can resolve the tensions into their horizontal components using the cosine function:

$$\begin{aligned} F_{horizontal, left} &= T_1 \cos(72^\circ) \\ F_{horizontal, right} &= T_2 \cos(28^\circ) \end{aligned}$$

For horizontal equilibrium, these must be equal:

$$T_1 \cos(72^\circ) = T_2 \cos(28^\circ)$$

Setting up this equation correctly earns you the first mark (**M1**).

steps.title: Rearrange for the ratio
steps.content:
To find the ratio $\frac{T_1}{T_2}$, we rearrange the equilibrium equation by dividing both sides by $T_2$ and by $\cos(72^\circ)$:

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{\cos(28^\circ)}{\cos(72^\circ)}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final value
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the values for the cosines and solve. Using your calculator, you will find:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{T_1}{T_2} &= \frac{0.8829...}{0.3090...} \\ \frac{T_1}{T_2} &\approx 2.86 \end{aligned}$$

Providing the final calculated value to a reasonable number of significant figures earns the second mark (**A1**).

gdc.instructions:
1. Ensure the calculator is in Degree mode.
2. Enter the fraction: cos(28) / cos(72)
3. Execute the calculation.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [MODE] and ensure 'DEGREE' is highlighted.
2. Press [2ND] [QUIT] to return to the home screen.
3. Type: cos(28) / cos(72)
4. Press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions:
1. Click the wrench icon (Settings) and select 'Degrees'.
2. Type the expression into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the ratio of the cosines using degree mode.

methods.label: Triangle of Forces and Sine Rule

steps.title: Visualize the force triangle
steps.content:
In physics, when three forces acting on a point are in **equilibrium**, they can be represented as a closed vector triangle. This is covered in **Topic A.2: Forces and momentum**. 

We have three forces: the tension $T_1$, the tension $T_2$, and the weight of the mass $mg$ acting vertically downwards. By placing these vectors tip-to-tail, we form a triangle where the lengths represent the magnitudes of the forces.

steps.title: Determine the interior angles
steps.content:
To use the Sine Rule, we need the internal angles of our triangle. Since the weight ($mg$) is vertical and the ceiling is horizontal, they are perpendicular ($90^\circ$).

- The angle between $T_1$ and the vertical is $90^\circ - 72^\circ = 18^\circ$.
- The angle between $T_2$ and the vertical is $90^\circ - 28^\circ = 62^\circ$.

In our closed triangle, these represent the angles opposite the forces $T_2$ and $T_1$ respectively.

highlights.text: angles between the ropes and the ceiling are 72° and 28°

steps.title: Apply the Sine Rule
steps.content:
The Sine Rule states that for any triangle, the ratio of a side length to the sine of its opposite angle is constant. For our force triangle, this means:

$$\frac{T_1}{\sin(62^\circ)} = \frac{T_2}{\sin(18^\circ)}$$

This allows us to relate the two tensions directly without needing to calculate the weight $mg$.

steps.title: Calculate the final ratio
steps.content:
Now, we rearrange the equation to find the ratio $\frac{T_1}{T_2}$:

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{\sin(62^\circ)}{\sin(18^\circ)}$$

Using a calculator:

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} \approx \frac{0.8829}{0.3090} \approx 2.86$$

Note: This is equivalent to the markscheme's result of $\frac{\cos(28^\circ)}{\cos(72^\circ)}$ because $\sin(90^\circ - \theta) = \cos(\theta)$.

gdc.instructions: Set angle units to Degrees. Enter sin(62) / sin(18) and evaluate.

ti84.instructions: Ensure the calculator is in DEGREE mode. Press [SIN] [6] [2] [)] [/] [SIN] [1] [8] [)] [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Switch to 'Degree' mode in the settings. Type sin(62)/sin(18).

calculator.description: Calculate the ratio of the sines of the angles.

parts.content: The mass $m$ is $20\ \mathrm{kg}$. Determine the numerical values of $T_1$ and $T_2$.
parts.markscheme:
- State the vertical equilibrium equation: $T_1 \sin(72^\circ) + T_2 \sin(28^\circ) = mg$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Substitute values ($m = 20$, $g = 9.81$) and ratio ($T_1 = 2.86 T_2$): $(2.86T_2) \sin(72^\circ) + T_2 \sin(28^\circ) = 196.2$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Solve for $T_2$: $3.19T_2 = 196.2 \Rightarrow T_2 = 61.5\ \mathrm{N}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Calculate $T_1$: $T_1 = 2.86 \times 61.5 = 176\ \mathrm{N}$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Resolve horizontal components
steps.content:
To find the tensions using the **Resolving into Components** approach, we first look at the horizontal forces. Since the system is in equilibrium (Topic A.2), the net horizontal force must be zero.

The horizontal components of the tensions are:
- $T_1$ (left): $T_1 \cos(72^\circ)$
- $T_2$ (right): $T_2 \cos(28^\circ)$

Setting them equal gives us a relationship between $T_1$ and $T_2$:
$$T_1 \cos(72^\circ) = T_2 \cos(28^\circ)$$
$$T_1 = T_2 \frac{\cos(28^\circ)}{\cos(72^\circ)} \approx 2.86 T_2$$

steps.title: Calculate the weight
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the downward force acting on the mass, which is its weight ($W$). From the data booklet, we use the formula for weight (derived from Newton's Second Law):

$$W = mg$$

Given the mass is $20\text{ kg}$ and using $g = 9.81\text{ m s}^{-2}$ (as used in the markscheme):
$$W = 20 \times 9.81 = 196.2\text{ N}$$

steps.title: Set up vertical equilibrium
steps.content:
Now we look at the vertical components. Both ropes pull upward, and their combined vertical force must balance the weight. The vertical components are:
- $T_1$ (up): $T_1 \sin(72^\circ)$
- $T_2$ (up): $T_2 \sin(28^\circ)$

Our vertical equilibrium equation is:
$$T_1 \sin(72^\circ) + T_2 \sin(28^\circ) = mg$$

Substituting our known values:
$$T_1 \sin(72^\circ) + T_2 \sin(28^\circ) = 196.2$$

highlights.text: Determine the numerical values of T1 and T2

steps.title: Solve for T2
steps.content:
We can now substitute the relationship $T_1 = 2.86 T_2$ into our vertical equation to solve for $T_2$:

$$(2.86 T_2) \sin(72^\circ) + T_2 \sin(28^\circ) = 196.2$$

Factor out $T_2$:
$$T_2 (2.86 \sin(72^\circ) + \sin(28^\circ)) = 196.2$$
$$T_2 (2.72 + 0.47) = 196.2$$
$$3.19 T_2 = 196.2$$
$$T_2 \approx 61.5\text{ N}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the expression directly in the main calculation screen. Ensure the mode is set to 'Degrees'.

ti84.instructions:
1. Calculate 2.86*sin(72) + sin(28)
2. Divide 196.2 by the result

desmos.instructions: Type the equation or calculate the parts step-by-step.

calculator.description: Calculate the coefficient and solve for T2

steps.title: Calculate T1
steps.content:
Finally, substitute the value of $T_2$ back into our horizontal relationship to find $T_1$:

$$T_1 = 2.86 \times 61.5$$
$$T_1 \approx 176\text{ N}$$

ti84.instructions: Multiply 2.86 by 61.5

calculator.description: Multiply T2 by the ratio 2.86

steps.title: Final Answers
steps.content:
The numerical values for the tensions are:
- $T_1 = 176\text{ N}$
- $T_2 = 61.5\text{ N}$

steps.title: Calculate the Weight
steps.content:
First, we determine the weight ($W$) of the $20\text{ kg}$ mass. Weight is the force due to gravity acting on the mass, found using the formula from the data booklet:

$$W = mg$$

Substituting the given values:

$$W = 20 \times 9.81 = 196.2\text{ N}$$

*Note: While the data booklet lists $g = 9.8\text{ m s}^{-2}$, the IB markscheme often uses $9.81\text{ m s}^{-2}$ for higher precision in intermediate steps.*

steps.title: Identify Angles for Lami's Theorem
steps.content:
To use Lami's Theorem, we need the angles between the three forces ($T_1$, $T_2$, and $W$) at the equilibrium point.

1. **Angle opposite $W$**: This is the angle between the two ropes. Since they make angles of $72^\circ$ and $28^\circ$ with the horizontal ceiling, the interior angle is:
$$180^\circ - (72^\circ + 28^\circ) = 80^\circ$$

2. **Angle opposite $T_1$**: This is the angle between $T_2$ and the vertical weight $W$. Since $T_2$ is $28^\circ$ above the horizontal, the angle with the vertical is:
$$90^\circ + 28^\circ = 118^\circ$$

3. **Angle opposite $T_2$**: This is the angle between $T_1$ and $W$. Since $T_1$ is $72^\circ$ above the horizontal, the angle with the vertical is:
$$90^\circ + 72^\circ = 162^\circ$$

steps.title: Apply Lami's Theorem
steps.content:
Lami's Theorem relates the magnitude of forces in equilibrium to the sines of the angles opposite them:

$$\frac{T_1}{\sin(118^\circ)} = \frac{T_2}{\sin(162^\circ)} = \frac{W}{\sin(80^\circ)}$$

Substitute the value of $W$ we calculated:

$$\frac{T_1}{\sin(118^\circ)} = \frac{T_2}{\sin(162^\circ)} = \frac{196.2}{\sin(80^\circ)}$$

steps.title: Solve for Tension T2
steps.content:
We can now solve for $T_2$ by rearranging the ratio:

$$T_2 = 196.2 \times \frac{\sin(162^\circ)}{\sin(80^\circ)}$$

Using a calculator, we find:

$$T_2 \approx 61.5\text{ N}$$

steps.title: Solve for Tension T1
steps.content:
Similarly, solve for $T_1$ using the other ratio:

$$T_1 = 196.2 \times \frac{\sin(118^\circ)}{\sin(80^\circ)}$$

Calculation gives:

$$T_1 \approx 176\text{ N}$$
