questions.specification:
A student drops a steel ball into two viscous liquids, labelled Liquid 1 and Liquid 2. In one liquid, the drag force is proportional to velocity; in the other, it is proportional to velocity squared. The ball’s motion is recorded using a high-speed camera. Assume that the density of steel is much larger than the density of liquids.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/db54d14c-2fb2-4b80-9812-d24909094d4f)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.1 Kinematics > A.1.4 Fluid resistance (qualitative only)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.4 Fluid resistance (qualitative only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Central focus is resistive/drag forces in fluids with different dependences on speed (linear vs quadratic), terminal velocity behavior, and how to distinguish drag models from motion/graphs.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.2 Forces and momentum > A.2.1 Newton's Laws of Motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.1 Newton's Laws of Motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses Newton’s 2nd law to relate forces (weight, drag, negligible upthrust) to acceleration; part (e) explicitly asks to compute resistive force from estimated acceleration.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.1 Kinematics > A.1.1 Describing motion quantitatively and qualitatively
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.1 Describing motion quantitatively and qualitatively
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Interpreting and extracting quantities from v–t graphs (terminal speed estimates) and describing qualitative changes of velocity/acceleration over time; also measuring velocity/acceleration from video data.

parts.content: Explain why the buoyant force acting on the ball may be neglected in this experiment.
parts.markscheme:
- State that steel is much denser than the liquids, so buoyant force is small compared to weight <marks>R1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the forces
steps.content:
To understand why we can neglect the buoyant force, we need to compare it to the weight of the steel ball. 

Both forces depend on the volume ($V$) of the ball and the acceleration due to gravity ($g$):
- The **Weight** ($W$) depends on the density of steel ($\rho_{s}$): 
$$W = \rho_{s} V g$$
- The **Buoyant Force** ($B$) depends on the density of the liquid ($\rho_{l}$), as per Archimedes' Principle:
$$B = \rho_{l} V g$$

steps.title: Compare force magnitudes
steps.content:
The question provides a critical assumption: the density of steel is much greater than the density of the liquids. 

Since both forces share the same $V$ and $g$, the ratio of the forces is simply the ratio of their densities:
$$\frac{B}{W} = \frac{\rho_{l}}{\rho_{s}}$$

Given that $\rho_{s} \gg \rho_{l}$, it follows that $W \gg B$. This means the weight is significantly larger than the buoyant force.

highlights.text: the density of steel is much larger than the density of liquids

steps.title: Final justification
steps.content: Because the buoyant force is such a tiny fraction of the weight, it has a negligible effect on the overall acceleration and motion of the ball. Therefore, for the purpose of this experiment, we can treat it as zero without significantly affecting our results.

parts.content: Describe qualitatively how and why the velocity of the ball changes after being dropped.
parts.markscheme:
- State that initially, the ball accelerates due to unbalanced weight force <marks>A1</marks>
- State that as speed increases, resistive force increases <marks>A1</marks>
- State that eventually, resistive force balances weight and the ball reaches terminal velocity <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Initial acceleration
steps.content:
When the steel ball is first dropped, its initial velocity is zero. At this moment, there is no drag force acting on it. The only significant force is its weight ($W$), which acts downwards. According to Newton's second law, this unbalanced force causes the ball to accelerate downwards.

This concept is part of **Topic A.2: Forces and momentum**. You can find the relevant formula in the Mechanics section of your data booklet:

$$F = ma$$

highlights.text: initially, the ball accelerates due to unbalanced weight force

steps.title: Effect of speed
steps.content: As the ball gains speed, it begins to experience a resistive force from the liquid, known as drag. The problem states that this drag force is proportional to either $v$ or $v^2$. In both liquids, as the velocity ($v$) increases, the magnitude of the resistive force also increases.

highlights.text: as speed increases, resistive force increases

steps.title: Reaching terminal velocity
steps.content:
The net force ($F_{net}$) on the ball is the difference between the constant weight and the increasing drag force:

$$F_{net} = W - D$$

As drag increases, the net force decreases, which means the acceleration of the ball also decreases. Eventually, the drag force becomes equal in magnitude to the weight. At this point, the net force is zero ($F_{net} = 0$), the acceleration is zero, and the ball continues to fall at a constant speed called **terminal velocity**.

highlights.text: eventually, resistive force balances weight and the ball reaches terminal velocity

steps.title: Initial acceleration at the start
steps.content:
When the ball is first released, its initial velocity is zero. At this moment, there is no drag force acting on it because resistive forces depend on speed. The only significant force acting is the weight of the ball, which is an **unbalanced force**.

According to Newton’s second law from your data booklet:

$$F = ma$$

Because the net force is at its maximum (equal to the weight), the acceleration is also at its maximum (approximately $g$). On a velocity-time graph, this would be represented by the **initial gradient** being at its steepest.

steps.title: Decreasing gradient as speed increases
steps.content:
As the ball gains speed, the resistive (drag) force increases. The question mentions that this force is proportional to either $v$ or $v^2$, but in both cases, as $v$ gets larger, the upward drag force grows.

This upward force opposes the downward weight, reducing the net force ($F_{net} = W - F_{drag}$). Since $F_{net}$ is decreasing, the acceleration ($a = \frac{F_{net}}{m}$) must also decrease. 

In terms of our graphical analysis, the **gradient of the velocity-time curve decreases** over time, causing the graph to curve and become less steep.

highlights.text: drag force is proportional to velocity

steps.title: Reaching terminal velocity
steps.content:
Eventually, the ball reaches a speed where the upward resistive force exactly balances the downward weight. At this point, the net force is zero:

$$F_{net} = 0 \implies a = 0$$

When the acceleration becomes zero, the velocity stops changing. This constant speed is known as the **terminal velocity**. 

On the velocity-time graph, the gradient becomes zero, and the curve reaches a **horizontal asymptote**. This indicates that the ball will continue to move at this steady speed for the remainder of its fall through the liquid.

parts.content: Suggest a method to measure the velocity and acceleration of the sinking ball.
parts.markscheme:
- Suggest using high-speed video and motion tracking software to determine position over time <marks>M1</marks>
- State to differentiate position–time data numerically to get velocity and acceleration <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze position with software
steps.content:
To measure the ball's motion precisely, we first import the footage from the **high-speed camera** into digital motion tracking software (such as Tracker). 

We set a reference scale in the software using a known distance in the video (like a ruler next to the container). By marking the center of the ball in each successive frame, the software generates a set of data points for position ($s$) relative to time ($t$).

This aligns with Topic **A.1 Kinematics**, where we describe motion quantitatively.

steps.title: Calculate velocity and acceleration
steps.content:
Once we have the position-time ($s$–$t$) data, we determine the velocity and acceleration through **numerical differentiation**:

1.  **Velocity ($v$):** The software calculates the gradient (slope) of the position-time graph. 
    $$v = \frac{\Delta s}{\Delta t}$$
2.  **Acceleration ($a$):** The software calculates the gradient of the resulting velocity-time graph (the second derivative of position).
    $$a = \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t}$$

In an IB exam, you earn the first mark for suggesting the software to find position over time and the second mark for stating that you differentiate this data to find $v$ and $a$.

gdc.instructions: In the Graphing menu, plot your position function. Use the 'Trace' or 'Analysis' tool to find 'dy/dx' at a specific x-value (time) to get velocity.

ti84.instructions: To find the derivative at a point: Press [MATH], select 8:nDeriv(. Enter the function, the variable (X), and the specific time value. For example: nDeriv(4.9X², X, 2).

desmos.instructions: Type the position function, e.g., f(x) = 4.9x^2. Then type f'(2) to find the velocity at 2 seconds.

calculator.description: While tracking software often does this automatically, you can use a GDC to find the instantaneous rate of change (derivative) at a specific point if you have a regression equation for the motion.

steps.title: Establish the scale
steps.content: To measure the ball's motion manually, we first need to record it using a **high-speed camera**. We must place a physical ruler or scale next to the container. By comparing the ball's position in each frame to this scale, we can convert measurements from the screen (pixels or cm) into real-world displacement ($s$) in meters.

steps.title: Determine time intervals
steps.content:
Next, identify the time interval ($\Delta t$) between consecutive frames. This is found using the camera's frame rate (fps):

$$\Delta t = \frac{1}{\text{frame rate}}$$

For example, if the camera records at $1000$ fps, the time between each frame is $0.001$ seconds. This relates to **Topic A.1 Kinematics**, where we describe motion using time and displacement.

steps.title: Calculate velocity
steps.content:
To find the velocity ($v$), measure the displacement ($\Delta s$) of the ball between two consecutive frames. The average velocity for that interval is the change in position divided by the change in time:

$$v = \frac{\Delta s}{\Delta t}$$

By repeating this for every pair of frames, we create a list of velocity values over time.

steps.title: Calculate acceleration
steps.content:
Finally, calculate the acceleration ($a$) by finding the change in velocity ($\Delta v$) between successive velocity intervals. This process is essentially numerical differentiation:

$$a = \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t}$$

This method allows us to see how the velocity and acceleration change as the ball experiences fluid resistance in the different liquids.

parts.content: Use the graph to estimate the terminal velocity for each liquid. Justify your estimates.
parts.markscheme:
- Estimate that Liquid 1 levels off at about $v_t = 1.80\,\mathrm{m\,s^{-1}}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Estimate that Liquid 2 levels off at about $v_t = 1.40\,\mathrm{m\,s^{-1}}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Define terminal velocity graphically
steps.content:
In physics, terminal velocity ($v_t$) occurs when the drag force and buoyancy exactly balance the weight of the object. According to Newton's Second Law, if the net force is zero, the acceleration is also zero:

$$F_{net} = ma = 0$$

On a velocity-time graph, acceleration is represented by the gradient (slope) of the curve. Therefore, terminal velocity is reached when the gradient becomes zero, which appears as a **horizontal asymptote** where the curve levels off.

steps.title: Analyze Liquid 1
steps.content:
Looking at the curve for **Liquid 1**, we can see it rises rapidly and then starts to flatten out as time increases. 

By following the curve to the right side of the graph (around $t = 0.5\text{ s}$ to $0.6\text{ s}$), we can see it stays at a constant level. Looking at the y-axis scale, where each small grid division represents $0.1\text{ m s}^{-1}$, the curve levels off three divisions above $1.5\text{ m s}^{-1}$.

$$v_{t1} \approx 1.80\text{ m s}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Analyze Liquid 2
steps.content:
Now, let's look at the curve for **Liquid 2**. This curve levels off much earlier and at a lower velocity than Liquid 1.

Following the curve for Liquid 2, it becomes horizontal at approximately $t = 0.3\text{ s}$. On the y-axis, this horizontal line aligns with the fourth small grid division above $1.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$.

$$v_{t2} \approx 1.40\text{ m s}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Justify the estimates
steps.content:
To earn full marks, you must justify these values. The justification is based on the **Asymptotic Graphical Analysis**: 

1.  Identify that the curves reach a constant velocity as time increases.
2.  State that the horizontal asymptote indicates the point where acceleration is zero ($a = 0$), meaning the forces are balanced and terminal velocity has been reached.

steps.title: Define terminal velocity
steps.content:
Terminal velocity occurs when the drag force equals the weight of the ball, resulting in zero acceleration. On a velocity-time graph, this is represented by the horizontal asymptote where the gradient (slope) of the curve becomes zero.

This concept is a key part of **Topic A.1 Kinematics** and **Topic A.2 Forces and momentum**, where we study how fluid resistance affects motion.

highlights.text: estimate the terminal velocity for each liquid

steps.title: Estimate Liquid 2
steps.content:
Looking at the curve for Liquid 2, we can see that it reaches a steady state relatively quickly. By approximately $t = 0.35\text{ s}$, the curve has flattened out.

If we trace this horizontal section back to the vertical axis, the velocity is exactly at the $1.40\text{ m s}^{-1}$ mark.

$$v_{t2} \approx 1.40\text{ m s}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Extrapolate for Liquid 1
steps.content:
Liquid 1 has not yet reached a constant velocity within the $0.50\text{ s}$ shown. At $t = 0.50\text{ s}$, the velocity is approximately $1.72\text{ m s}^{-1}$ and the gradient is still slightly positive.

Using **Trend Extrapolation**, we extend the curve following its current trajectory. Since the gradient is decreasing (the curve is "bending" downwards), we look for the value where the line would naturally become horizontal. Following this trend, the curve is projected to level off at the next major grid line.

$$v_{t1} \approx 1.80\text{ m s}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Justify the estimates
steps.content:
To earn full marks, we must justify our values based on the graph's behavior:

1.  **Liquid 2:** The velocity becomes constant (the gradient is zero) at $1.40\text{ m s}^{-1}$ before the end of the recorded time.
2.  **Liquid 1:** While it is still accelerating at $0.50\text{ s}$, the decreasing gradient suggests it is approaching a limit. Extrapolating this trend indicates a terminal velocity of approximately $1.80\text{ m s}^{-1}$.

parts.content: Estimate the acceleration of the ball in Liquid 2 at $t = 0.5\,\mathrm{s}$, then calculate the resistive force acting on it at that moment. The mass of the ball is $m_b = 20\,\mathrm{g}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Estimate acceleration using gradient of graph near $t = 0.5\,\mathrm{s}$: $a = \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t} = \frac{1.15 - 0.0}{1.0} \approx 1.15\,\mathrm{m\,s^{-2}}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate resistive force: $F_{\text{resistive}} = mg - ma = 0.020 \times (9.81 - 1.15) \approx 0.173\,\mathrm{N}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

parts.content: Describe how the student could determine which liquid has linear drag and which has quadratic drag.
parts.markscheme:
- State Newton's second law rearranged for drag: $ma = mg - kv$ or $ma = mg - kv^2$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Describe plotting $a$ vs $v$ and $a$ vs $v^2$ from numerical estimates <marks>M1</marks>
- Conclude that a linear relationship indicates the type of drag: linear for $a$ vs $v$, quadratic for $a$ vs $v^2$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Model the forces
steps.content:
To distinguish between the two drag models, we first apply Newton's Second Law. The net force on the ball is the weight minus the drag force (ignoring buoyancy since the ball's density is much higher than the liquid's).

According to the data booklet, the net force is:
$$F = ma$$

Substituting the drag models, we get two possible equations for the ball's motion:
1. **Linear drag:** $ma = mg - kv$  
2. **Quadratic drag:** $ma = mg - kv^2$

steps.title: Extract motion data
steps.content:
Using the high-speed camera data, the student can determine the displacement of the ball over time. From this, they should calculate:
- **Instantaneous velocity ($v$):** The rate of change of displacement.
- **Instantaneous acceleration ($a$):** The rate of change of velocity.

These values are calculated for multiple points during the ball's descent in both Liquid 1 and Liquid 2.

steps.title: Plot for linearization
steps.content:
To use the graphical linearization approach, we rearrange the motion equations into a linear form ($y = mx + c$):

$$\begin{aligned} a &= -\left(\frac{k}{m}\right)v + g \\ a &= -\left(\frac{k}{m}\right)v^2 + g \end{aligned}$$

For each liquid, the student should create two separate plots:
1. **Acceleration ($a$) vs. Velocity ($v$)**
2. **Acceleration ($a$) vs. Velocity squared ($v^2$)**

steps.title: Identify the drag model
steps.content:
Finally, analyze the shape of the graphs to conclude:
- If the graph of **$a$ vs $v$** is a straight line, the liquid follows the **linear drag** model ($F_d \propto v$).
- If the graph of **$a$ vs $v^2$** is a straight line, the liquid follows the **quadratic drag** model ($F_d \propto v^2$).

The liquid that produces a linear fit for a specific graph identifies which model it belongs to.

steps.title: Apply Newton's Second Law
steps.content:
To understand the forces acting on the ball, we start with Newton’s second law. As the ball falls, the net force is the difference between its weight and the drag force. 

We are told to assume the density of the steel ball is much larger than the density of the liquids, which means we can ignore the buoyancy force. The equation of motion is:

$$F_{net} = ma$$
$$mg - F_D = ma$$

Rearranging for the drag force $F_D$:

$$F_D = m(g - a)$$ 

This provides the foundation for comparing our two drag models: linear ($F_D = kv$) and quadratic ($F_D = kv^2$).

highlights.text: density of steel is much larger than the density of liquids

steps.title: Derive the Constant Ratios
steps.content:
To use the **Numerical Constant Analysis** approach, we need to isolate the drag constant $k$ (divided by mass $m$, which is also constant) for each model.

For the **Linear Model** ($F_D = kv$):
$$kv = m(g - a) \implies \frac{g - a}{v} = \frac{k}{m}$$

For the **Quadratic Model** ($F_D = kv^2$):
$$kv^2 = m(g - a) \implies \frac{g - a}{v^2} = \frac{k}{m}$$

If a model is correct, the calculated ratio on the left should remain constant regardless of the ball's velocity.

steps.title: Perform Numerical Analysis
steps.content:
Using the high-speed camera footage, the student can determine the velocity ($v$) and acceleration ($a$) of the ball at several different times during its descent.

For each liquid, the student should calculate two sets of values:
1. The ratio $\frac{g - a}{v}$ for various points.
2. The ratio $\frac{g - a}{v^2}$ for the same points.

Note: Use $g = 9.8 \text{ m s}^{-2}$ from the data booklet for these calculations.

steps.title: Identify Drag Type
steps.content:
Finally, compare the results for each liquid:

*   If the ratio $\frac{g - a}{v}$ remains approximately the same for different velocities, that liquid exhibits **linear drag**.
*   If the ratio $\frac{g - a}{v^2}$ remains approximately the same, that liquid exhibits **quadratic drag**.

By checking which ratio produces a consistent 'constant' value, the student successfully identifies the drag model for each liquid.

questions.specification: A $1500 \, \text{kg}$ car accelerates uniformly from $5.0 \, \text{m s}^{-1}$ to $15.0 \, \text{m s}^{-1}$ in $4.0\ \text{s}$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.2 Forces and momentum > A.2.2 Linear momentum and impulse
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.2 Linear momentum and impulse
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (c) and (d) explicitly ask for impulse and its relationship to change in momentum (impulse–momentum theorem).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.2 Forces and momentum > A.2.1 Newton's Laws of Motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.1 Newton's Laws of Motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) requires net force from mass and acceleration using Newton’s second law, and the context is dynamics of a car.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.1 Kinematics > A.1.2 Equations of motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.2 Equations of motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) computes uniform acceleration from change in velocity over time, using constant-acceleration kinematics relationships.

parts.content: Calculate the acceleration of the car.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute values into the acceleration formula: $a = \frac{v - u}{t} = \frac{15.0 - 5.0}{4.0}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the final answer: $a = 2.5\ \text{m s}^{-2}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

parts.content: Determine the net force acting on the car.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute values into Newton's Second Law: $F = ma = 1500 \times 2.5$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the final answer: $F = 3750\ \text{N}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the given information
steps.content:
First, let's list the values we know from the question. This helps us choose the right equations from the IB data booklet. 

- Mass of the car ($m$) = $1500 \, \text{kg}$
- Initial velocity ($u$) = $5.0 \, \text{m s}^{-1}$
- Final velocity ($v$) = $15.0 \, \text{m s}^{-1}$
- Time interval ($t$) = $4.0 \, \text{s}$

This problem involves both motion (Topic A.1 Kinematics) and the cause of motion (Topic A.2 Forces).

steps.title: Calculate the acceleration
steps.content:
To find the force using Newton's second law ($F = ma$), we first need the acceleration ($a$). Since the car accelerates uniformly, we can use the kinematic equation:

$$v = u + at$$

Rearranging for $a$:

$$\begin{aligned} a &= \frac{v - u}{t} \\ a &= \frac{15.0 - 5.0}{4.0} \\ a &= 2.5 \, \text{m s}^{-2} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: In the Run-Matrix menu, enter (15 - 5) / 4 and press EXE.

ti84.instructions: Enter (15 - 5) / 4 on the home screen and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type (15 - 5) / 4 into a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the acceleration by finding the change in velocity over time.

steps.title: Apply Newton's Second Law
steps.content:
Now that we have the acceleration, we can calculate the net force ($F$) using Newton's Second Law of Motion:

$$F = ma$$

Substituting our values into the equation (this step earns you the first mark, **M1**):

$$\begin{aligned} F &= 1500 \times 2.5 \\ F &= 3750 \, \text{N} \end{aligned}$$

The final answer is $3750 \, \text{N}$ (this earns the final mark, **A1**).

gdc.instructions: In the Run-Matrix menu, enter 1500 * 2.5 and press EXE.

ti84.instructions: Enter 1500 * 2.5 on the home screen and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 1500 * 2.5 into a new expression line.

calculator.description: Multiply the mass by the calculated acceleration to find the force.

steps.title: Identify the given values
steps.content:
First, let's list the information provided in the question. We have the mass of the car ($m$), the initial velocity ($u$), the final velocity ($v$), and the time interval ($\Delta t$) over which the acceleration occurs.

$$\begin{aligned} m &= 1500 \text{ kg} \\ u &= 5.0 \text{ m s}^{-1} \\ v &= 15.0 \text{ m s}^{-1} \\ \Delta t &= 4.0 \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$

This problem falls under **Topic A.2 Forces and momentum**, where we look at how forces change the motion of objects.

steps.title: Apply Impulse-Momentum Theorem
steps.content:
The Impulse-Momentum Theorem states that the impulse ($J$) applied to an object is equal to its change in momentum ($\Delta p$). From the data booklet, we can relate net force ($F$) to the rate of change of momentum:

$$F = \frac{\Delta p}{\Delta t}$$

Since momentum is defined as $$p=mv$$, the change in momentum for a constant mass is:

$$\Delta p = m(v - u)$$ 

Combining these, we get the expression for the net force:

$$F = \frac{m(v - u)}{\Delta t}$$

steps.title: Calculate the net force
steps.content:
Now, substitute the known values into our equation to solve for the net force ($F$):

$$\begin{aligned} F &= \frac{1500 \times (15.0 - 5.0)}{4.0} \\ F &= \frac{1500 \times 10.0}{4.0} \\ F &= \frac{15000}{4.0} \end{aligned}$$

$$F = 3750 \text{ N}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the main screen: (1500 * (15 - 5)) / 4

desmos.instructions: Type the formula directly to find the result.

calculator.description: Calculate the net force using the impulse-momentum relationship.

steps.title: Final answer and marks
steps.content:
The net force acting on the car is **$3750 \text{ N}$**.

In an IB exam, you would earn marks as follows:
- **M1**: Correct substitution of values into a valid physics relationship (even if using $F=ma$ after calculating $a$, the result is the same).
- **A1**: The correct final answer with appropriate units ($N$).

steps.title: Calculate the total displacement
steps.content:
To use the Work-Energy Theorem, we first need to find the distance ($s$) the car traveled while accelerating. Since the acceleration is uniform, we can use the kinematic equation for displacement from the **Mechanics** section of your data booklet:

$$s = \frac{u+v}{2}t$$

Substituting the given values:
$$\begin{aligned} s &= \frac{5.0 + 15.0}{2} \times 4.0 \\ s &= \frac{20.0}{2} \times 4.0 \\ s &= 40.0 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Determine change in kinetic energy
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the total work done on the car, which is equal to its change in kinetic energy ($\Delta E_k$). Using the formula for kinetic energy from the data booklet:

$$E_{k} = \frac{1}{2}mv^{2}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta E_k &= \frac{1}{2}mv^2 - \frac{1}{2}mu^2 \\ \Delta E_k &= \frac{1}{2}(1500)(15.0^2) - \frac{1}{2}(1500)(5.0^2) \\ \Delta E_k &= 168750 - 18750 \\ \Delta E_k &= 150000 \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve for net force
steps.content:
The work done ($W$) by the net force is also defined as the product of force and displacement. Assuming the force acts in the direction of motion ($\theta = 0^\circ$):

$$W = Fs$$

Since $W = \Delta E_k$, we can set up the equation to solve for $F$:

$$\begin{aligned} Fs &= \Delta E_k \\ F(40.0) &= 150000 \\ F &= \frac{150000}{40.0} \\ F &= 3750 \text{ N} \end{aligned}$$

In the markscheme, you earn **M1** for showing a valid method to find force (like substituting mass and acceleration, or energy and distance) and **A1** for the correct final answer of $3750 \text{ N}$.

gdc.instructions: Perform the division of the energy change by the distance.

ti84.instructions: Enter the division calculation on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the division expression to find the net force.

calculator.description: Calculate the final force by dividing the work done by the displacement.

parts.content: Calculate the impulse experienced by the car.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute values into the impulse formula: $\text{Impulse} = \Delta p = m(v - u) = 1500 \times (15.0 - 5.0)$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the final answer with correct units: $\text{Impulse} = 15\,000 \text{ N s}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Accept $\text{kg m s}^{-1}$ as an equivalent unit.</note>

steps.title: Identify the given information
steps.content:
To calculate the impulse using the change in momentum approach, we first list the values provided in the question:

- Mass ($m$) = $1500 \, \text{kg}$
- Initial velocity ($u$) = $5.0 \, \text{m s}^{-1}$
- Final velocity ($v$) = $15.0 \, \text{m s}^{-1}$

In **Topic A.2: Forces and momentum**, we learn that impulse is defined as the change in linear momentum.

steps.title: Apply impulse-momentum theorem
steps.content:
The impulse ($J$) is equal to the change in momentum ($\Delta p$). Since momentum is $p = mv$, the change in momentum for an object with constant mass is:

$$J = \Delta p = m(v - u)$$

Substituting our known values into this equation gives us the first mark according to the markscheme.

steps.title: Calculate the numerical value
steps.content:
Now we perform the calculation:

$$\begin{aligned} J &= 1500 \times (15.0 - 5.0) \\ J &= 1500 \times 10.0 \\ J &= 15\,000 \end{aligned}$$

You can use your GDC to quickly verify this result.

gdc.instructions: Enter the expression into the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into a calculation cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the impulse using the change in momentum formula.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The impulse experienced by the car is $15\,000 \, \text{N s}$. 

Remember that the units for impulse can be expressed as Newton-seconds ($\text{N s}$) or kilogram meters per second ($\text{kg m s}^{-1}$), as both are equivalent and accepted in the IB markscheme.

steps.title: Define the approach
steps.content:
To find the impulse using the **Force and Time Approach**, we will follow a three-step process:
1. Find the constant acceleration of the car.
2. Use Newton's Second Law to find the resultant force.
3. Use the definition of impulse as the product of force and time.

This method uses concepts from **Topic A.1 (Kinematics)** and **Topic A.2 (Forces and momentum)**.

steps.title: Calculate the acceleration
steps.content:
First, we find the acceleration ($a$) using the kinematic equation for uniform acceleration:

$$v = u + at$$

Rearranging for $a$:

$$a = \frac{v - u}{t}$$

Substituting the given values:

$$a = \frac{15.0 - 5.0}{4.0} = 2.5 \text{ m s}^{-2}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the expression into the calculation menu.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into a cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the acceleration.

highlights.text: accelerates uniformly from $5.0 \, \text{m s}^{-1}$ to $15.0 \, \text{m s}^{-1}$ in $4.0\ \text{s}$

steps.title: Determine the resultant force
steps.content:
Now, we use Newton's Second Law to calculate the constant resultant force ($F$) acting on the car:

$$F = ma$$

Substituting the mass and the acceleration we just calculated:

$$F = 1500 \times 2.5 = 3750 \text{ N}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply the mass by the calculated acceleration.

ti84.instructions: Enter the product of mass and acceleration.

calculator.description: Calculate the force.

steps.title: Calculate the impulse
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the impulse ($J$) using the formula relating force and time interval:

$$J = F\Delta t$$

Substituting our values:

$$J = 3750 \times 4.0 = 15\,000 \text{ N s}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the product of force and time.

ti84.instructions: Multiply the force by the time interval.

calculator.description: Calculate the final impulse.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The impulse experienced by the car is **$15\,000 \text{ N s}$** (or $1.5 \times 10^4 \text{ N s}$).

Note that Impulse can also be expressed in units of $\text{kg m s}^{-1}$, as it is equivalent to the change in momentum.

parts.content: Explain how impulse is related to the car's change in momentum.
parts.markscheme:
- State that impulse is equal to the change in momentum: $\text{Impulse} = \Delta p$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Relate impulse to force and time: Impulse is the product of the average force and the time interval ($F \cdot t = \Delta p$). <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Define the relationship
steps.content:
To explain the relationship, we first use the **Impulse-Momentum Theorem**. This principle states that the impulse ($J$) applied to an object is exactly equal to the resulting change in its momentum ($\Delta p$). 

This concept is a key part of **Topic A.2: Forces and momentum**. In the context of the car, any impulse delivered by the engine's force over the 4-second interval results in a proportional change in its momentum.

$$J = \Delta p$$

steps.title: Relate impulse to force
steps.content:
From your IB data booklet, we also see that impulse is defined as the product of the average resultant force ($F$) and the time interval ($\Delta t$) during which it acts:

$$J = F \Delta t$$

Combining these two ideas, we get the relationship:

$$F \Delta t = \Delta p$$

This shows that the car's momentum increases because a force was applied for a specific duration ($4.0\text{ s}$). This is how the two marks are earned: identifying that impulse equals the change in momentum and relating it to the product of force and time.

steps.title: Verify the momentum change
steps.content:
While the question asks for an explanation, we can verify this by calculating the change in momentum ($\Delta p = m(v - u)$) for the car. Since $m = 1500\text{ kg}$, $u = 5.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$, and $v = 15.0\text{ m s}^{-1}$:

$$\Delta p = 1500(15.0 - 5.0) = 15000\text{ kg m s}^{-1}$$

This $15000\text{ kg m s}^{-1}$ is the total impulse that was delivered to the car.

gdc.instructions: Calculate the product of mass and the difference in velocities.

ti84.instructions: Enter the mass times the change in velocity.

desmos.instructions: Evaluate the change in momentum expression.

calculator.description: Calculate the change in momentum to verify the impulse value.

steps.title: Define impulse
steps.content:
In IB Physics, we define impulse ($J$) as the product of the average force ($F$) applied to an object and the time interval ($\Delta t$) during which that force acts. 

From the data booklet, we have:
$$J = F \Delta t$$

steps.title: Apply Newton's Second Law
steps.content:
Newton's Second Law states that the resultant force acting on an object is proportional to the rate of change of its linear momentum. This is covered in **Topic A.2: Forces and momentum**. 

Mathematically, we express this as:
$$F = \frac{\Delta p}{\Delta t}$$

Where $\Delta p$ represents the change in momentum and $\Delta t$ is the time taken for that change.

steps.title: Link impulse to momentum
steps.content:
To see the relationship, we can rearrange the Newton's Second Law equation by multiplying both sides by the time interval ($\Delta t$):

$$\begin{aligned} F &= \frac{\Delta p}{\Delta t} \\ F \cdot \Delta t &= \Delta p \end{aligned}$$

Since we know from Step 0 that $J = F \Delta t$, we can substitute $J$ into the equation.

steps.title: State the final relationship
steps.content:
By combining these two ideas, we conclude that the impulse applied to the car is exactly equal to its change in momentum:

$$\text{Impulse} = \Delta p$$

In an exam, you earn one mark for stating that impulse equals the change in momentum and another mark for showing the relationship using the product of force and time ($F \Delta t = \Delta p$).

questions.specification:
A cyclist coasts down a hill at a terminal speed. Which of the following is true about the magnitude of the component of the weight down the slope and the magnitude of the total resistive force?

| | Component of weight down the slope | Total resistive force |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| A | greater than the resistive force | smaller than the component of weight |
| B | equal to the resistive force | equal to the component of weight |
| C | smaller than the resistive force | greater than the component of weight |
| D | equal to the weight | zero |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.2 Forces and momentum > A.2.1 Newton's Laws of Motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.1 Newton's Laws of Motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks for the relationship between forces when an object moves at a constant (terminal) speed, which is a direct application of Newton's First Law of Motion (zero net force results in zero acceleration).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.1 Kinematics > A.1.4 Fluid resistance (qualitative only)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.4 Fluid resistance (qualitative only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The concept of terminal speed is specifically discussed in the context of fluid resistance and resistive forces balancing driving forces.

options.markscheme: This would result in an acceleration down the slope.

At terminal speed, the acceleration is zero. According to Newton's second law, the net force must be zero. Therefore, the magnitude of the component of the weight down the slope must be equal to the magnitude of the total resistive force acting up the slope.
This would result in a deceleration.
If the resistive force were zero, the cyclist would continue to accelerate; furthermore, the component of weight down the slope is only equal to the weight on a vertical drop (90° slope).

questions.specification: A box of mass $10.0\ \text{kg}$ is pulled across a horizontal surface by a rope at an angle of $30^\circ$ above the horizontal. The tension in the rope is $60.0\ \text{N}$. The coefficient of kinetic friction between the box and the surface is $0.25$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.2 Forces and momentum > A.2.1 Newton's Laws of Motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.1 Newton's Laws of Motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses Newton’s laws with multiple forces on a block (tension components, normal reaction, kinetic friction) to find net force and acceleration; also asks conceptual effect of changing rope angle on normal force.

parts.content: Calculate the horizontal component of the tension force.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute values into component formula: $F_{\text{horizontal}} = 60.0 \cos(30^\circ)$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate final value: $51.96\ \text{N}$ (or $52.0\ \text{N}$) <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

parts.content: Determine the normal force acting on the box.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate vertical component of tension: $F_{\text{vertical}} = 60.0 \sin(30^\circ) = 30.0\ \text{N}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Determine normal force using vertical equilibrium: $N = mg - F_{\text{vertical}} = 98.1 - 30.0 = 68.1\ \text{N}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the vertical forces
steps.content:
To find the normal force, we need to look at all the forces acting in the vertical direction. Since the box is moving horizontally and not lifting off the ground, it is in **vertical equilibrium**. This means the sum of the upward forces must equal the sum of the downward forces.

There are three vertical forces to consider:
1. The **weight** ($mg$) acting downwards.
2. The **normal force** ($R$ or $N$) acting upwards from the surface.
3. The **vertical component of the tension** ($T_y$) acting upwards.

steps.title: Calculate vertical tension component
steps.content:
The rope pulls at an angle, so only a portion of the $60.0\ \text{N}$ tension helps support the box's weight. We find this vertical component ($T_y$) using the sine function:

$$T_y = T \sin(\theta)$$

Substituting the given values:

$$T_y = 60.0 \times \sin(30^\circ) = 30.0\ \text{N}$$

gdc.instructions: Set angle mode to Degrees. Enter 60 * sin(30).

ti84.instructions: Ensure the calculator is in DEGREE mode. Press [6][0][*][SIN][3][0][ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Set to Degree mode using the wrench icon. Type 60 * sin(30).

calculator.description: Calculate the vertical component of the tension.

steps.title: Calculate the box weight
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the downward force of gravity (weight). Using $g = 9.81\ \text{m s}^{-2}$ (the standard value used in IB markschemes for more precision than the rounded $9.8$):

$$W = mg$$
$$W = 10.0 \times 9.81 = 98.1\ \text{N}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the weight of the box.

steps.title: Solve for normal force
steps.content:
Now we apply the principle of vertical equilibrium. The total upward force must equal the total downward force:

$$F_{\text{upward}} = F_{\text{downward}}$$
$$N + T_y = mg$$

Rearrange to solve for the normal force ($N$):

$$\begin{aligned} N &= mg - T_y \\ N &= 98.1 - 30.0 \\ N &= 68.1\ \text{N} \end{aligned}$$

This shows that because the rope is pulling upward, the surface doesn't have to push back as hard as it would if the box were just sitting there.

calculator.description: Calculate the final normal force.

parts.content: Calculate the acceleration of the box.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate friction force: $f = \mu N = 0.25 \times 68.1 = 17.025\ \text{N}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate net force: $F_{\text{net}} = 51.96 - 17.025 = 34.936...\ \text{N}$
- Calculate acceleration: $a = \frac{F_{\text{net}}}{m} = \frac{34.936...}{10.0} = 3.49\ \text{m\,s}^{-2}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Accept range 3.49–3.50 m s⁻² to account for rounding differences in intermediate steps (e.g., using rounded friction of 17.0 N yields 3.50 m s⁻²).</note>

steps.title: Calculate the Normal Force
steps.content:
The box is in vertical equilibrium, meaning the upward forces (Normal force $N$ and the vertical component of tension $T\sin\theta$) must balance the downward force (Weight $mg$).

Using the formula for weight from the data booklet:
$$\Delta E_{p}=mg\Delta h \implies W = mg$$

$$\begin{aligned} N + T\sin\theta &= mg \\ N &= mg - T\sin\theta \end{aligned}$$

Substituting the values:
$$\begin{aligned} N &= (10.0 \times 9.81) - (60.0 \times \sin 30^\circ) \\ N &= 98.1 - 30.0 = 68.1\text{ N} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the Friction Force
steps.content:
Kinetic friction is calculated using the coefficient of friction ($\mu$) and the normal force ($N$) we just found.

$$f = \mu N$$

$$\begin{aligned} f &= 0.25 \times 68.1 \\ f &= 17.025\text{ N} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate Horizontal Net Force
steps.content:
The net force in the horizontal direction ($F_{\text{net}}$) is the difference between the forward pulling component ($T\cos\theta$) and the opposing friction force ($f$).

$$\begin{aligned} F_{\text{net}} &= T\cos\theta - f \\ F_{\text{net}} &= (60.0 \times \cos 30^\circ) - 17.025 \\ F_{\text{net}} &= 51.961... - 17.025 \\ F_{\text{net}} &= 34.936...\text{ N} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve for Acceleration
steps.content:
Finally, we apply Newton's second law from the data booklet to find the acceleration ($a$):

$$F = ma$$

$$\begin{aligned} a &= \frac{F_{\text{net}}}{m} \\ a &= \frac{34.936...}{10.0} \\ a &= 3.4936... \approx 3.49\text{ m s}^{-2} \end{aligned}$$

According to the markscheme, values between $3.49$ and $3.50$ are accepted to account for intermediate rounding.

gdc.instructions: Enter the formula directly in the calculation menu. Check that the angle unit is set to degrees.

ti84.instructions: Type the expression into the home screen. Ensure you are in Degree mode (MODE > DEGREE).

desmos.instructions: Set the calculator to Degrees using the wrench icon, then enter the expression.

calculator.description: Calculate the final acceleration using all components.

steps.title: Analyze vertical equilibrium
steps.content:
To find the acceleration, we first need the normal force $R$. Since the box does not move vertically, the net force in the vertical direction is zero. The forces acting vertically are the weight ($mg$), the normal force ($R$), and the vertical component of the tension ($T \sin\theta$).

$$\sum F_y = R + T \sin\theta - mg = 0$$

Solving for the normal force $R$:
$$R = mg - T \sin\theta$$

steps.title: Define friction and net force
steps.content:
Using the equation for kinetic friction from your understanding of Topic A.2 (Forces), we know that $f = \mu R$. 

We also use Newton's Second Law for the horizontal direction, where the net force is the horizontal component of tension ($T \cos\theta$) minus the friction force ($f$):

$$F_{\text{net}} = T \cos\theta - f = ma$$

steps.title: Derive consolidated acceleration formula
steps.content:
Using the **Unified Algebraic Approach**, we substitute the expression for $R$ into the friction formula, and then substitute friction into the net force equation:

$$f = \mu(mg - T \sin\theta)$$

Now, substitute this into the horizontal motion equation:
$$T \cos\theta - \mu(mg - T \sin\theta) = ma$$

Finally, rearrange to solve for acceleration $a$:
$$a = \frac{T \cos\theta - \mu(mg - T \sin\theta)}{m}$$

steps.title: Calculate the numerical value
steps.content:
Now we substitute the known values into our consolidated formula:
- $m = 10.0\ \text{kg}$
- $T = 60.0\ \text{N}$
- $\theta = 30^\circ$
- $\mu = 0.25$
- $g = 9.8\ \text{m s}^{-2}$ (from the Physical Constants section)

$$a = \frac{60.0 \cos(30^\circ) - 0.25(10.0 \times 9.8 - 60.0 \sin(30^\circ))}{10.0}$$

gdc.instructions: In the Run-Matrix menu, enter the fraction and ensure the angle mode is degrees.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression into the home screen. Use the COS and SIN buttons (ensure MODE is set to DEGREE).

desmos.instructions: Type the formula directly into the expression bar. Use the settings (wrench icon) to switch to Degrees.

calculator.description: Calculate the acceleration using the derived algebraic expression.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
From the calculation:
$$a \approx 3.496... \text{ m s}^{-2}$$

Rounding to three significant figures (matching the precision of the given values), we get:
$$a = 3.50\ \text{m s}^{-2}$$

*(Note: If you use $g = 9.81\ \text{m s}^{-2}$, the result is $3.49\ \text{m s}^{-2}$. Both are accepted within the markscheme range).*

parts.content: Explain the effect of increasing the angle of the rope on the normal force.
parts.markscheme:
- State effect on vertical component: Increasing the angle increases the vertical component of tension <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain effect on normal force: This reduces the normal force (and consequently the friction) <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

questions.specification: A $5.0\ \mathrm{kg}$ sphere falls through a viscous fluid at terminal velocity. If its radius is $0.10\ \mathrm{m}$ and terminal speed is $2.0\ \mathrm{m\,s^{-1}}$, what is the viscosity $\eta$ of the fluid? Assume $g = 10\ \mathrm{m\,s^{-2}}$ and the buoyant force is negligible.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.1 Kinematics > A.1.4 Fluid resistance (qualitative only)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.4 Fluid resistance (qualitative only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses terminal velocity of a sphere in a viscous fluid to determine viscosity via drag (Stokes’ law), i.e., fluid resistance effects.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.2 Forces and momentum > A.2.1 Newton's Laws of Motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.1 Newton's Laws of Motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: At terminal velocity the net force is zero (weight balanced by viscous drag), applying Newton’s laws/force balance.

options.markscheme:
At terminal velocity, the gravitational force equals the viscous drag force (assuming negligible buoyancy):
$$mg = 6\pi\eta r v$$

Rearranging for viscosity $\eta$:
$$\eta = \frac{mg}{6\pi r v}$$

Substituting the given values ($m=5.0$, $g=10$, $r=0.10$, $v=2.0$):
$$\eta = \frac{5.0 \times 10}{6\pi \times 0.10 \times 2.0}$$

Calculating the result:
$$\eta = \frac{50}{1.2\pi} \approx 13.3\ \mathrm{Pa\cdot s}$$

The correct answer is $13\ \mathrm{Pa\cdot s}$.

parts.markscheme: - The product of the (average) force and the time interval for which it acts **OR** the change in momentum <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: A hockey puck of mass $0.16\text{ kg}$ slides across a frictionless surface at $15\text{ m s}^{-1}$. It strikes a wall and rebounds in the opposite direction at $12\text{ m s}^{-1}$. Calculate the magnitude of the change in momentum of the puck.
parts.markscheme:

- $4.3\text{ kg m s}^{-1}$ (or $\text{N s}$) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.markscheme:

parts.markscheme:

questions.specification: A skier of mass $70 \, \mathrm{kg}$ is skiing down a slope inclined at $25^{\circ}$ to the horizontal. The slope is $50 \, \mathrm{m}$ long. Assume no air resistance, and the coefficient of kinetic friction between the skis and the slope is $\mu_k=0.15$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.3 Work, energy and power > A.3.2 Work and energy transfer
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.2 Work and energy transfer
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (a) and (b) involve resolving weight along the slope and calculating work done against friction, directly using work/energy transfer concepts (work = force × distance).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.3 Work, energy and power > A.3.1 Conservation of energy
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.3.1 Conservation of energy
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) explicitly asks for an energy-considerations approach: gravitational potential energy converted to kinetic energy minus energy dissipated by friction (non-conservative work).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.2 Forces and momentum > A.2.1 Newton's Laws of Motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.1 Newton's Laws of Motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Resolving forces on an incline (weight components, normal force) and using friction (F=μN) relies on Newtonian force analysis, even though the final speed is found via energy.

parts.content: Calculate the component of the skier's weight acting parallel to the slope.
parts.markscheme:
- State the expression for the parallel component of weight: $F_{\text{parallel}} = mg \sin(\theta)$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Substitute the values: $F_{\text{parallel}} = 70 \times 9.81 \times \sin(25^{\circ})$

- Calculate the final value: $F_{\text{parallel}} \approx 290 \, \mathrm{N}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the given values
steps.content: To calculate the parallel component of the weight, we first need to identify the mass of the skier and the angle of the slope provided in the question. We also need the gravitational field strength ($g$) from the physical constants section of your data booklet.

steps.title: Recall the resolution formula
steps.content:
When an object is on an inclined plane, its weight acts vertically downwards. We can resolve this weight into two components using trigonometry. The component acting parallel to the slope (the one trying to pull the skier down) is given by:

$$F_{\text{parallel}} = mg \sin(\theta)$$ 

This is a standard application of resolving forces in **Topic A.2 Forces and momentum**.

steps.title: Substitute and calculate
steps.content:
Now, substitute the known values into our formula:

$$\begin{aligned} F_{\text{parallel}} &= 70 \times 9.8 \times \sin(25^{\circ}) \\ F_{\text{parallel}} &\approx 289.92 \, \mathrm{N} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions:
1. Set angle mode to Degrees.
2. Enter the calculation: 70 * 9.8 * sin(25).

ti84.instructions:
1. Ensure the calculator is in Degree mode (Press [MODE], highlight 'DEGREE', press [ENTER]).
2. Multiply the mass, gravity, and the sine of the angle: 70 * 9.8 * sin(25).

desmos.instructions:
1. Click the wrench icon (Settings) and select 'Degrees'.
2. Type the expression: 70 * 9.8 * sin(25).

calculator.description: Calculate the parallel component of weight.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Rounding to two significant figures (as given in the question for the angle and coefficient of friction), we get:

$$F_{\text{parallel}} = 290 \, \mathrm{N}$$

In an exam, you earn one mark for the correct expression or substitution and one mark for the final value with units.

steps.title: Calculate the total weight
steps.content:
First, we determine the skier's weight ($W$), which is the force due to gravity acting vertically downwards. We use the formula for weight:

$$W = mg$$

From the data booklet, the acceleration due to gravity is $g = 9.8 \, \mathrm{m \, s^{-2}}$. Substituting the given mass:

$$W = 70 \times 9.8 = 686 \, \mathrm{N}$$

steps.title: Construct the force triangle
steps.content:
To find the component of weight parallel to the slope, we construct a right-angled force triangle. In this construction:
- The total weight ($W = 686 \, \mathrm{N}$) is the **hypotenuse**, acting vertically down.
- One leg of the triangle is the component **perpendicular** to the slope ($F_{\perp}$).
- The other leg is the component **parallel** to the slope ($F_{\parallel}$).

Geometry shows that the angle between the weight vector and the perpendicular component is equal to the incline of the slope, which is $25^{\circ}$.

steps.title: Apply the sine ratio
steps.content:
In our right-angled force triangle, the parallel component ($F_{\parallel}$) is the side **opposite** to the $25^{\circ}$ angle. We use the definition of sine:

$$\sin(\theta) = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{hypotenuse}}$$

Substituting our values:

$$\sin(25^{\circ}) = \frac{F_{\parallel}}{mg}$$

Rearranging to solve for the parallel component:

$$F_{\parallel} = mg \sin(25^{\circ})$$

steps.title: Calculate the parallel component
steps.content:
Finally, we substitute the numerical values into the expression to find the magnitude of the force parallel to the slope:

$$F_{\parallel} = 686 \times \sin(25^{\circ})$$

$$F_{\parallel} \approx 290 \, \mathrm{N}$$

According to the markscheme, stating the expression $mg \sin(\theta)$ earns the first mark, and providing the final calculated value earns the second mark.

gdc.instructions: Set the angle mode to Degrees. Enter the calculation: 70 * 9.8 * sin(25).

ti84.instructions: Ensure the calculator is in Degree mode. Press [MODE], select 'DEGREE', then [ENTER]. Then, type 70 * 9.8 * sin(25) and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Click the wrench icon to ensure the 'Degrees' setting is selected. Type: 70 * 9.8 * sin(25).

calculator.description: Calculate the parallel component of weight using the sine function.

parts.content: Determine the work done against friction as the skier moves down the slope.
parts.markscheme:
The frictional force is given by $F_{\text{friction}} = \mu_k N$.

- Calculate the normal force $N$: $N = mg \cos(\theta) = 70 \times 9.81 \times \cos(25^{\circ}) \approx 622.4 \, \mathrm{N}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate the frictional force: $F_{\text{friction}} = 0.15 \times 622.4 \approx 93.4 \, \mathrm{N}$

- Calculate the work done against friction: $W_{\text{friction}} = F_{\text{friction}} \times d = 93.4 \times 50 \approx 4670 \, \mathrm{J}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the forces
steps.content:
To find the work done against friction, we first need to determine the magnitude of the friction force acting on the skier. Because the skier is on an inclined slope, the normal force ($N$)—which determines the friction—is not simply equal to the weight, but rather the component of the weight perpendicular to the slope.

From the question, we have:
- Mass ($m$) = $70 \, \mathrm{kg}$
- Slope angle ($\theta$) = $25^{\circ}$
- Distance ($d$) = $50 \, \mathrm{m}$
- Coefficient of kinetic friction ($\mu_k$) = $0.15$

steps.title: Calculate the normal force
steps.content:
The normal force $N$ is the component of the weight ($mg$) acting perpendicular to the inclined plane. Using the geometry of the slope:

$$N = mg \cos(\theta)$$

Using the gravitational acceleration $g = 9.8 \, \mathrm{m \, s^{-2}}$ from the data booklet:

$$N = 70 \times 9.8 \times \cos(25^{\circ})$$

$$N \approx 621.7 \, \mathrm{N}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 70 * 9.8 * cos(25) into the main calculation screen.

desmos.instructions: Type '70 * 9.8 * cos(25)' and ensure the angle mode is set to Degrees.

calculator.description: Calculate the normal force $N = 70 \times 9.8 \times \cos(25^\circ)$. Ensure your calculator is in Degree mode.

steps.title: Calculate the friction force
steps.content:
Now we calculate the kinetic friction force ($F_f$) using the coefficient of friction $\mu_k$. The formula is:

$$F_f = \mu_k N$$

Substituting our values:

$$F_f = 0.15 \times 621.7$$

$$F_f \approx 93.26 \, \mathrm{N}$$

ti84.instructions: Press [0][.][1][5] [*] [ANS] [ENTER]

calculator.description: Calculate friction force by multiplying $\mu_k$ and $N$.

steps.title: Determine the work done
steps.content:
The work done against friction ($W_f$) is the product of the friction force and the distance traveled along the slope. We use the formula for work from the data booklet:

$$W = Fs \cos(\theta)$$

Since we are calculating the work done *against* friction, and friction acts parallel to the path of motion (displacement), we simply multiply the force magnitude by the distance:

$$W_f = F_f \times d$$

$$W_f = 93.26 \times 50$$

$$W_f \approx 4663 \, \mathrm{J}$$

Rounding to two significant figures (following IB convention for the given $\mu_k = 0.15$), we get **$4700 \, \mathrm{J}$**. (Note: If using $g = 9.81$, the result is $\approx 4670 \, \mathrm{J}$).

ti84.instructions: Press [ANS] [*] [5][0] [ENTER]

calculator.description: Calculate total work by multiplying force and distance.

parts.content: Using energy considerations, calculate the skier's speed at the bottom of the slope if they start from rest.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the vertical height $h$ or initial potential energy: $h = 50 \sin(25^{\circ}) \approx 21.13 \, \mathrm{m}$ OR $E_{\text{initial}} = mgd \sin(\theta)$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate the initial potential energy: $E_{\text{initial}} = 70 \times 9.81 \times 21.13 \approx 14510 \, \mathrm{J}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Apply the work-energy principle: $E_{\text{kinetic}} = E_{\text{initial}} - W_{\text{friction}} = 14510 - 4670 = 9840 \, \mathrm{J}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate the speed: $v = \sqrt{\frac{2 E_{\text{kinetic}}}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 9840}{70}} \approx 16.8 \, \mathrm{m\,s^{-1}}$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

methods.label: Potential Energy and Work against Friction

steps.title: Identify the energy approach
steps.content:
To find the skier's speed, we will use the principle of conservation of energy. Since there is friction, the total mechanical energy is not conserved; instead, the initial potential energy is converted into kinetic energy and work done against friction.

From the data booklet, we'll need the formulas for potential energy, work, and kinetic energy:

$$\Delta E_{p}=mg\Delta h$$
$$W=Fs\cos\theta$$
$$E_{k}=\frac{1}{2}mv^{2}$$

steps.title: Calculate vertical height
steps.content:
The skier travels $50 \, \mathrm{m}$ down a $25^{\circ}$ slope. We need the vertical height change ($h$) to calculate the potential energy.

Using trigonometry:
$$h = 50 \cdot \sin(25^{\circ})$$
$$h \approx 21.13 \, \mathrm{m}$$

ti84.instructions: Ensure the calculator is in DEGREE mode. Press [5][0][SIN][2][5][ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Set the calculator to Degrees and type 50 * sin(25).

calculator.description: Calculate the vertical height using the sine function.

steps.title: Determine initial potential energy
steps.content:
Now we calculate the gravitational potential energy ($E_p$) at the top of the slope. We use $g = 9.8 \, \mathrm{m \, s^{-2}}$ as provided in the physical constants.

$$\begin{aligned} E_p &= mgh \\ E_p &= 70 \cdot 9.8 \cdot 21.13 \\ E_p &\approx 14495 \, \mathrm{J} \end{aligned}$$

*(Note: Using $g=9.81$ would yield $\approx 14510 \, \mathrm{J}$ as seen in the markscheme, but we follow the data booklet constant $9.8$)*.

calculator.description: Calculate the initial potential energy.

steps.title: Calculate work against friction
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the energy lost to friction. 

1. Find the normal force ($R$): $R = mg \cos(25^{\circ})$
2. Find the friction force ($F_f$): $F_f = \mu_k R = 0.15 \cdot 70 \cdot 9.8 \cdot \cos(25^{\circ})$
3. Calculate work ($W_f$): $W_f = F_f \cdot d$

$$\begin{aligned} W_f &= 0.15 \cdot 70 \cdot 9.8 \cdot \cos(25^{\circ}) \cdot 50 \\ W_f &\approx 4663 \, \mathrm{J} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the work done by friction.

steps.title: Find final speed
steps.content:
The final kinetic energy ($E_k$) is the initial potential energy minus the work lost to friction:

$$E_k = E_p - W_f = 14495 - 4663 = 9832 \, \mathrm{J}$$

Now, solve for $v$ using $E_{k}=\frac{1}{2}mv^{2}$:

$$\begin{aligned} v &= \sqrt{\frac{2E_k}{m}} \\ v &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 9832}{70}} \\ v &\approx 16.8 \, \mathrm{m \, s^{-1}} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Solve for the final speed v.

steps.title: Identify the Net Work-Energy Theorem
steps.content:
To find the skier's speed, we can use the **Net Work-Energy Theorem**. This theorem states that the net work done on an object is equal to its change in kinetic energy:

$$W_{\text{net}} = \Delta E_k = E_{k, \text{final}} - E_{k, \text{initial}}$$

Since the skier **starts from rest**, the initial kinetic energy is $0$. Therefore, the work done by the net force acting along the slope will equal the final kinetic energy at the bottom. This concept is covered in **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**.

steps.title: Calculate gravity's parallel component
steps.content:
First, we find the component of the skier's weight acting parallel to the slope (downwards). Using trigonometry:

$$F_{\text{parallel}} = mg \sin(\theta)$$

Using the values from the question and the data booklet ($g = 9.8 \text{ m s}^{-2}$):

$$\begin{aligned} F_{\text{parallel}} &= 70 \times 9.8 \times \sin(25^{\circ}) \\ F_{\text{parallel}} &\approx 289.9 \text{ N} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Set angle mode to degrees and compute the product.

ti84.instructions: Ensure mode is in DEGREES. Enter the expression.

desmos.instructions: Set calculator to degrees and type the expression.

calculator.description: Calculate the force component parallel to the slope.

steps.title: Calculate the kinetic friction force
steps.content:
Friction acts against the motion. The force of kinetic friction depends on the normal force ($R$), which is the component of weight perpendicular to the slope ($mg \cos(\theta)$):

$$\begin{aligned} F_f &= \mu_k R \\ F_f &= \mu_k mg \cos(\theta) \end{aligned}$$

Substituting the known values:

$$\begin{aligned} F_f &= 0.15 \times 70 \times 9.8 \times \cos(25^{\circ}) \\ F_f &\approx 93.3 \text{ N} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Compute the friction force magnitude in degree mode.

ti84.instructions: Multiply the coefficient by mass, gravity, and the cosine of the angle.

desmos.instructions: Enter the formula for kinetic friction.

calculator.description: Calculate the magnitude of the friction force.

steps.title: Determine Net Force and Work
steps.content:
The net force ($F_{\text{net}}$) is the difference between the driving force (gravity) and the resistive force (friction). Then, we multiply by the displacement ($s = 50 \text{ m}$) to find the net work:

$$\begin{aligned} F_{\text{net}} &= F_{\text{parallel}} - F_f \\ F_{\text{net}} &\approx 289.9 - 93.3 = 196.6 \text{ N} \end{aligned}$$

Now, apply the work formula from the data booklet:

$$W = Fs \cos \theta$$

Since the net force is parallel to the displacement, $\cos(0) = 1$:

$$\begin{aligned} W_{\text{net}} &= F_{\text{net}} \times s \\ W_{\text{net}} &= 196.6 \times 50 \approx 9830 \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply the net force by the distance.

ti84.instructions: Calculate the difference between the previous results and multiply by 50.

calculator.description: Calculate total net work done.

steps.title: Solve for final speed
steps.content:
Finally, we set the net work equal to the kinetic energy formula from the data booklet to solve for $v$:

$$\begin{aligned} W_{\text{net}} &= \frac{1}{2}mv^2 \\ 9830 &= \frac{1}{2} \times 70 \times v^2 \\ v^2 &= \frac{2 \times 9830}{70} \\ v &= \sqrt{280.86} \approx 16.8 \text{ m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

**Marking Breakdown:**
- **M1**: Correct method for parallel component of weight or height.
- **A1**: Correct calculation of work or energy values.
- **M1**: Applying the Work-Energy principle ($E_k = W_{\text{net}}$).
- **A1**: Final speed correctly rounded to 3 significant figures.

ti84.instructions: Take the square root of (2 * Net Work / mass).

calculator.description: Solve for the final velocity.

questions.specification:
A block of mass $0.40 \text{ kg}$ slides across a horizontal frictionless surface and hits a spring buffer. The block rebounds in the opposite direction. The following data are available for the collision:

| Parameter | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Speed immediately before impact | $6.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ |
| Speed immediately after impact | $4.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ |
| Duration of contact with the buffer | $0.080 \text{ s}$ |

What is the magnitude of the average force exerted by the buffer on the block?

options.markscheme: This value is obtained by subtracting the speeds ($6.0 - 4.0 = 2.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$) instead of accounting for the change in direction of the velocity vector.
This value is obtained by using only the initial momentum change ($0.40 \times 6.0 = 2.4$) and dividing by the time, or incorrectly considering only one part of the motion.
This value is a distractor based on an arithmetic error or incorrect speed summation.

The average force $F$ is given by $F = \frac{\Delta p}{\Delta t}$. Taking the initial direction as positive, $\Delta v = v - u = (-4.0) - (+6.0) = -10 \text{ m s}^{-1}$. The magnitude of the change in momentum is $|\Delta p| = m|\Delta v| = 0.40 \times 10 = 4.0 \text{ kg m s}^{-1}$. Therefore, $F = \frac{4.0}{0.080} = 50 \text{ N}$.

![Thrust graph](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-economics-e0c568af-a453-4bed-b2ce-0bde459b9414-1771937587718/b5db53b7-d16b-40b7-9a89-6bfc283be4b2.jpeg)

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses the impulse–momentum theorem (net force as rate of change of momentum) and requires calculating impulse from an F–t graph (area under curve) to find change in momentum.

parts.content: State the relationship between the net force acting on an object and its momentum.
parts.markscheme: - The net force is the rate of change of momentum: $$F = \frac{\Delta p}{\Delta t}$$ (or equivalent in words). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Determine the total impulse delivered to the probe during the firing sequence from $t = 0$ to $t = 40 \text{ s}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Recognize that impulse is the area under the force-time graph. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Calculate the area (trapezoid or sum of triangles and rectangle):
  - $\text{Impulse} = \frac{1}{2} \times (10 + 30) \times 400 + \dots$ (using trapezoid: $\frac{1}{2}(a+b)h = \frac{1}{2}(20 + 40) \times 400$)
  - $\text{Impulse} = 0.5 \times 60 \times 400 = 12,000 \text{ N s}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: The probe was initially traveling at a constant speed of $120 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ in the $x$-direction. The thrust was applied in the $y$-direction. Calculate the final speed of the probe after the thruster has finished firing.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate initial momentum in the $x$-direction:
  $$p_x = m v_x = 250 \times 120 = 30,000 \text{ kg m s}^{-1}$$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Recognize impulse is the change in momentum in the $y$-direction:
  $$p_y = \Delta p_y = 12,000 \text{ kg m s}^{-1}$$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Calculate magnitude of resultant momentum:
  $$p_{\text{final}} = \sqrt{p_x^2 + p_y^2} = \sqrt{30,000^2 + 12,000^2} \approx 32,311 \text{ kg m s}^{-1}$$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Calculate final speed:
  $$v_{\text{final}} = \frac{p_{\text{final}}}{m} = \frac{32,311}{250} \approx 129 \text{ m s}^{-1}$$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
A student investigates how the extension of a spring depends on the applied force. The spring is suspended vertically and a range of masses is added to it. For each mass, the student measures the extension of the spring from its natural (unstretched) length. The results are shown below.

| Force $F(\mathrm{~N})$ | Extension $x(\mathrm{~cm})$ |
| :---: | :---: |
| 0.50 | 1.0 |
| 1.00 | 2.1 |
| 1.50 | 3.1 |
| 2.00 | 4.0 |
| 2.50 | 4.7 |
| 3.00 | 5.2 |
| 3.50 | 5.5 |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic A - Space, time and motion > A.2 Forces and momentum > A.2.1 Newton's Laws of Motion
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.1 Newton's Laws of Motion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses force vs extension data for a spring to identify proportionality (Hooke’s law region) and determine spring constant from gradient; this is an application of forces and equilibrium/elastic response under Newtonian mechanics.

parts.content: Sketch a graph of extension $x$ against force $F$.
parts.markscheme:
- Accurate axes, appropriate scaling. <marks>[1]</marks>
- Correct trend (curve begins linear, flattens slightly at higher forces). <marks>[1]</marks>

steps.title: Identify the Axes
steps.content:
In IB Physics, when asked to plot a graph of **A against B**, the first variable (A) is plotted on the vertical $y$-axis and the second variable (B) is plotted on the horizontal $x$-axis.

For this question, we are plotting **extension $x$ against force $F$**. 
- $y$-axis: Extension $x$ (cm)
- $x$-axis: Force $F$ (N)

steps.title: Choose Appropriate Scales
steps.content:
To get the first mark, your axes must be scaled so that the data covers at least half of the graph paper.

- **Force ($F$):** Values range from $0.50$ to $3.50\text{ N}$. A scale from $0$ to $4.0\text{ N}$ with $0.5\text{ N}$ increments works perfectly.
- **Extension ($x$):** Values range from $1.0$ to $5.5\text{ cm}$. A scale from $0$ to $6.0\text{ cm}$ with $1.0\text{ cm}$ increments is ideal.

steps.title: Plot the Data Points
steps.content:
Using the **Point-by-Point Plotting** approach, we carefully mark each coordinate from the table onto our grid. 

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|} \hline F \text{ (N)} & x \text{ (cm)} \\ \hline 0.50 & 1.0 \\ 1.00 & 2.1 \\ 1.50 & 3.1 \\ 2.00 & 4.0 \\ 2.50 & 4.7 \\ 3.00 & 5.2 \\ 3.50 & 5.5 \\ \hline \end{array}$$

Notice that as the force increases, the amount of additional extension for every $0.50\text{ N}$ begins to decrease.

steps.title: Draw the Best Fit
steps.content:
The final step is to draw a single, smooth line of best fit through the points. 

1. For the first four points ($0.50\text{ N}$ to $2.00\text{ N}$), the relationship is nearly linear (a straight line).
2. Beyond $2.00\text{ N}$, the gradient starts to decrease, so you must transition into a smooth curve that "flattens out" towards the top right.

This indicates the spring is reaching its limit of proportionality.

gdc.instructions: In the Run-Matrix menu, calculate the slopes to see the change in trend.

ti84.instructions: Calculate the gradient between the first two points and the last two points to compare.

desmos.instructions: Enter the points into a table to visualize the curve.

calculator.description: You can check the trend by calculating the gradient (change in extension / change in force) between points to see it decreasing.

highlights.text: Correct trend (curve begins linear, flattens slightly at higher forces)

steps.title: Define the axes
steps.content:
In a graph of "extension $x$ against force $F$", the first variable ($x$) goes on the vertical $y$-axis and the second variable ($F$) goes on the horizontal $x$-axis. Be sure to include the units given in the data table.

- **$y$-axis**: Extension $x$ (cm)
- **$x$-axis**: Force $F$ (N)

steps.title: Identify the initial trend
steps.content:
Let's look at the first few data points to find the relationship at low forces. For $F = 0.50$, $1.00$, and $1.50$, we calculate the ratio $\frac{x}{F}$:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{1.0}{0.50} &= 2.0 \\ \frac{2.1}{1.00} &= 2.1 \\ \frac{3.1}{1.50} &\approx 2.07 \end{aligned}$$

The ratio is approximately constant ($x \approx 2F$), indicating a linear relationship through the origin for the first half of the data.

ti84.instructions: Enter 1.0 / 0.50, then 2.1 / 1.00, then 3.1 / 1.50 in the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Create a table with the first three points and observe they lie nearly on the line y = 2x.

calculator.description: Calculate ratios to check for linearity

steps.title: Analyze the late trend
steps.content:
Now, observe the increments in extension ($\Delta x$) for every $0.50$ N increase in force:

- $F=1.5 \rightarrow 2.0$: $\Delta x = 4.0 - 3.1 = 0.9$ cm
- $F=2.0 \rightarrow 2.5$: $\Delta x = 4.7 - 4.0 = 0.7$ cm
- $F=2.5 \rightarrow 3.0$: $\Delta x = 5.2 - 4.7 = 0.5$ cm
- $F=3.0 \rightarrow 3.5$: $\Delta x = 5.5 - 5.2 = 0.3$ cm

Since the extension increments are **decreasing** as the force increases, the gradient of the graph is decreasing. The curve will start to "flatten out."

gdc.instructions: Calculate the difference between each subsequent x-value.

ti84.instructions: Perform subtractions for consecutive extension values: 4.0 - 3.1, 4.7 - 4.0, 5.2 - 4.7, 5.5 - 5.2.

desmos.instructions: Plot all points from the table to see the curve's behavior.

calculator.description: Calculate differences in extension

steps.title: Sketch the final graph
steps.content:
To earn full marks, combine these observations into a single sketch:

1.  **Axes**: Draw an $L$-shape with $x$ on the vertical axis and $F$ on the horizontal axis. Label them with units.
2.  **Origin**: Ensure your curve starts at $(0,0)$.
3.  **Initial Section**: Draw a straight line from the origin up to about $F = 1.5$.
4.  **Final Section**: Gradually curve the line so that it becomes less steep (flattens) as $F$ increases toward $3.5$.

This behavior shows that the spring is reaching its limit and providing less additional extension for equal increments of force.

parts.content: Describe the overall shape of the graph.
parts.markscheme: - The graph is initially linear, but curves and becomes less steep at higher forces. <marks>[1]</marks>

steps.title: Analyze Force Increments
steps.content: To describe the graph's shape without plotting it, we look at how the extension $x$ changes for every equal increase in force $F$. Notice that the force in the table increases by a constant step of $0.50\text{ N}$ every time.

steps.title: Calculate Extension Increments
steps.content:
Let's calculate the change in extension ($\Delta x$) for each $0.50\text{ N}$ step:

$$\begin{aligned} 0.50 \to 1.00 \text{ N}&: \Delta x = 2.1 - 1.0 = 1.1 \text{ cm} \\ 1.00 \to 1.50 \text{ N}&: \Delta x = 3.1 - 2.1 = 1.0 \text{ cm} \\ 1.50 \to 2.00 \text{ N}&: \Delta x = 4.0 - 3.1 = 0.9 \text{ cm} \end{aligned}$$

For the first few steps, the extension increases by roughly the same amount ($1.1 \approx 1.0 \approx 0.9$). This indicates the graph is **initially linear** (a straight line).

gdc.instructions: Enter the subtractions into the calculation app.

ti84.instructions: Enter the subtractions directly into the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the subtractions into separate expression bars.

calculator.description: Verify the first three extension increments.

steps.title: Identify the Curvature
steps.content:
Now let's look at the increments for the higher forces:

$$\begin{aligned} 2.00 \to 2.50 \text{ N}&: \Delta x = 4.7 - 4.0 = 0.7 \text{ cm} \\ 2.50 \to 3.00 \text{ N}&: \Delta x = 5.2 - 4.7 = 0.5 \text{ cm} \\ 3.00 \to 3.50 \text{ N}&: \Delta x = 5.5 - 5.2 = 0.3 \text{ cm} \end{aligned}$$

The amount of extra extension added for each $0.50\text{ N}$ is clearly decreasing ($0.7 \to 0.5 \to 0.3$). This means the graph stops being straight and starts to **curve**.

To describe the shape of the graph, we should visualize plotting the data points with Force ($F$) on the horizontal x-axis and Extension ($x$) on the vertical y-axis. 

We look for patterns in how the extension changes as the force increases in regular intervals of $0.50\text{ N}$.

Let's examine the first four data points. As the force increases by $0.50\text{ N}$ each time, the extension increases by roughly the same amount ($1.0\text{ cm}$ to $1.1\text{ cm}$).

$$\begin{aligned} \text{At } 0.50\text{ N} &\rightarrow 1.0\text{ cm} \\ \text{At } 1.00\text{ N} &\rightarrow 2.1\text{ cm} \\ \text{At } 1.50\text{ N} &\rightarrow 3.1\text{ cm} \\ \text{At } 2.00\text{ N} &\rightarrow 4.0\text{ cm} \end{aligned}$$

Since the extension is directly proportional to the force here, a sketch would show a **straight line** (linear) starting from near the origin.

For every $0.50\text{ N}$ added now, the extension grows by smaller and smaller amounts:
$$\begin{aligned} 4.7 - 4.0 &= 0.7\text{ cm} \\ 5.2 - 4.7 &= 0.5\text{ cm} \\ 5.5 - 5.2 &= 0.3\text{ cm} \end{aligned}$$

On a graph, this means the curve begins to flatten out, or become **less steep**, as it moves to the right.
